title: Henry VIII becomes king
description: Henry VIII ascended the throne at 17, launching one of England's most dramatic reigns, marked by six marriages, religious upheaval, and the break with Rome.

title: Luther's 95 Theses
description: Martin Luther's list of objections to Church abuses — particularly the sale of indulgences — sparked the Protestant Reformation and split Western Christianity permanently.

title: Cortés invades Mexico
description: Hernán Cortés led a small army of conquistadors that toppled the Aztec Empire within two years, opening Mexico to Spanish colonization.

title: Suleiman the Magnificent becomes Sultan
description: Under Suleiman's 46-year reign, the Ottoman Empire reached its greatest extent, stretching from Hungary to the Persian Gulf.

title: Siege of Vienna
description: The Ottoman army reached the gates of Vienna but failed to take the city, marking the high-water mark of Ottoman expansion into Western Europe.

title: English Reformation begins
description: Henry VIII's Act of Supremacy severed England from papal authority, making the king head of the Church of England and triggering a century of religious conflict.

title: Ivan IV crowned Tsar
description: Ivan the Terrible became the first ruler formally crowned Tsar of All Russia, expanding the realm dramatically while ruling with paranoid violence.

title: Peace of Augsburg
description: The treaty ended wars between Lutheran princes and the Catholic Emperor, establishing that each ruler could determine his territory's religion.

title: Elizabeth I becomes queen
description: Elizabeth I's 44-year reign brought political stability, cultural flourishing, and England's emergence as a Protestant maritime power.

title: Suleiman the Magnificent dies
description: The longest-reigning Ottoman sultan died on campaign in Hungary, leaving an empire at its peak but one that would soon begin a slow contraction.

title: Battle of Lepanto
description: A Holy League fleet decisively defeated the Ottoman navy off the coast of Greece — the first major Ottoman military defeat and a psychological shock to the Islamic world.

title: St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre
description: Catholic mobs, with royal approval, slaughtered thousands of Huguenot Protestants across France in a single night, shocking Protestant Europe.

title: Spanish Armada defeated
description: England's naval forces repelled Philip II's invasion fleet, cementing Protestant dominance in England and signaling the decline of Spanish naval supremacy.

title: Edict of Nantes
description: Henry IV granted French Protestants the right to practice their faith and hold public office, ending four decades of religious war — a landmark act of toleration.

title: James I becomes king
description: The Scottish King James VI united the English and Scottish crowns, beginning the Stuart dynasty and commissioning the King James Bible.

title: Romanov dynasty begins
description: The election of 16-year-old Michael Romanov as Tsar ended the Time of Troubles and founded the dynasty that would rule Russia until 1917.

title: Thirty Years' War begins
description: What began as a Protestant revolt in Bohemia became Europe's most destructive war, killing up to a third of the German population over 30 years.

title: Louis XIV becomes king
description: The 'Sun King' became the most powerful monarch in French history, reigning for 72 years and embodying absolute monarchy at its height.

title: Peace of Westphalia
description: The treaties that ended the Thirty Years' War established the modern system of sovereign nation-states and the principle of non-interference in internal affairs.

title: Charles I executed
description: After losing the English Civil War, Charles I was tried for treason and publicly beheaded — the first execution of a ruling European monarch by his own parliament.

title: Peter the Great becomes Tsar
description: Peter the Great's transformative reign modernized Russia along Western lines, building a new navy, founding St. Petersburg, and making Russia a European power.

title: Second Siege of Vienna
description: The Ottoman army's second and final attempt to capture Vienna was repelled by a combined Christian force led by Polish King Jan Sobieski, marking the turning point of Ottoman power in Europe.

title: Vasily III becomes Grand Prince
description: Vasily III completed the unification of the Russian principalities around Moscow, annexing Pskov, Smolensk, and Ryazan during his reign.

title: Selim I becomes Sultan
description: 'Selim the Grim' tripled the empire's size in 8 years by conquering Persia's western territories, Mamluk Egypt, and Syria, and assumed the title of Caliph.

title: Francis I becomes king
description: A patron of the arts who brought Leonardo da Vinci to France, Francis I was also a tireless rival of Emperor Charles V on the battlefield.

title: Charles I becomes king
description: Charles I — also Holy Roman Emperor Charles V — created the largest European empire since Rome, controlling Spain, the Low Countries, Naples, and the Americas.

title: Charles V becomes Emperor
description: The most powerful ruler in Christendom, Charles V simultaneously ruled Spain, the Holy Roman Empire, the Low Countries, and vast American territories.

title: Edward VI becomes king
description: The nine-year-old son of Henry VIII, Edward's brief reign accelerated Protestant reforms in England before his early death at 15.

title: Henry II becomes king
description: Henry II continued France's Renaissance brilliance while pursuing wars against the Habsburgs, dying in a jousting tournament in 1559.

title: Mary I becomes queen
description: England's first ruling queen reversed her father's religious reforms, restoring Catholicism and earning the epithet 'Bloody Mary' for burning Protestant heretics.

title: Philip II becomes king
description: Philip II presided over Spain's greatest imperial power, commanding the world's largest navy and the silver wealth of the Americas, while fighting Protestant rebellion on multiple fronts.

title: Ferdinand I becomes Emperor
description: Charles V's brother inherited the Austrian Habsburg lands and the Imperial title after Charles's abdication, reshaping the empire around Central Europe.

title: Francis II becomes king
description: Husband of Mary Queen of Scots, Francis II reigned only 17 months before dying of an ear infection, leaving France to his 10-year-old brother.

title: Charles IX becomes king
description: Charles IX was barely 10 when he took the throne; his reign was dominated by his mother Catherine de' Medici and culminated in the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre.

title: Maximilian II becomes Emperor
description: A moderate who privately sympathized with Protestantism, Maximilian II navigated the religious tensions of the empire without triggering open conflict.

title: Selim II becomes Sultan
description: Known as 'Selim the Sot,' the son of Suleiman was the first Ottoman sultan to delegate military command, presiding over Lepanto's defeat.

title: Henry III becomes king
description: The last Valois king, Henry III faced constant civil war between Catholics and Huguenots and was assassinated in 1589, ending his dynasty.

title: Murad III becomes Sultan
description: Murad III's reign saw the Ottoman Empire at near-maximum extent, but also the first signs of court intrigue and the weakening of the traditional system of governance.

title: Rudolf II becomes Emperor
description: Rudolf II moved the imperial court to Prague and became a patron of art and science, while his political incompetence deepened the empire's religious crisis.

title: Feodor I becomes Tsar
description: The pious and mentally limited son of Ivan the Terrible, Feodor I was effectively ruled by his regent Boris Godunov, who would succeed him.

title: Henry IV becomes king
description: A Protestant who converted to Catholicism to secure the throne ('Paris is worth a Mass'), Henry IV ended the Wars of Religion and began the Bourbon dynasty.

title: Mehmed III becomes Sultan
description: One of the last actively campaigning Ottoman sultans, Mehmed III had 19 brothers executed on accession day to eliminate rivals — a common but brutal Ottoman practice.

title: Boris Godunov becomes Tsar
description: A capable administrator who had ruled Russia for years as regent, Godunov's reign was undermined by famine, plague, and the appearance of pretenders to the throne.

title: Philip III becomes king
description: Philip III's reign saw the expulsion of the Moriscos and a gradual economic decline as Spain's silver revenues diminished and corruption spread.

title: Ahmed I becomes Sultan
description: Ahmed I broke with tradition by not executing his brothers on accession, and built the famous Blue Mosque in Istanbul as his great architectural legacy.

title: Louis XIII becomes king
description: Son of the assassinated Henry IV, Louis XIII's reign was dominated by Cardinal Richelieu, who centralized French power and made France Europe's leading land power.

title: Matthias becomes Emperor
description: Rudolf II's brother seized the throne after Rudolf proved unable to govern, but Matthias also failed to resolve the religious tensions that would erupt into the Thirty Years' War.

title: Mustafa I becomes Sultan
description: Mentally unstable and probably suffering from schizophrenia, Mustafa I reigned only briefly before being deposed in favor of his nephew Osman II.

title: Osman II becomes Sultan
description: The reforming young sultan attempted to reduce the Janissaries' power and was murdered by them in 1622 — the first time Janissaries killed a sitting sultan.

title: Ferdinand II becomes Emperor
description: A devout Catholic, Ferdinand II's attempt to re-Catholicize Bohemia triggered the Thirty Years' War, the most destructive conflict Europe had seen since the Black Death.

title: Philip IV becomes king
description: Philip IV's reign coincided with Spain's slow decline as a great power, losing Portugal and parts of the Netherlands during the Thirty Years' War.

title: Mustafa I becomes Sultan again
description: After Osman II's murder by the Janissaries, the deposed and mentally ill Mustafa I was briefly restored, ruling for another year before final deposition.

title: Murad IV becomes Sultan
description: One of the last strong Ottoman sultans, Murad IV banned coffee, alcohol, and tobacco on pain of death, and personally enforced the laws by patrolling Istanbul at night.

title: Charles I becomes king
description: Charles I's reign was defined by conflict with Parliament over taxation and royal authority, ultimately leading to civil war and his execution.

title: Ferdinand III becomes Emperor
description: Ferdinand III's primary achievement was negotiating the Peace of Westphalia, ending the Thirty Years' War that had devastated the empire.

title: Ibrahim becomes Sultan
description: Known as 'Ibrahim the Mad,' his erratic and cruel rule ended when the Janissaries had him deposed and executed after eight years.

title: Alexis I becomes Tsar
description: The second Romanov tsar presided over Russia's annexation of Ukraine and the great church schism, and was the father of Peter the Great.

title: Mehmed IV becomes Sultan
description: Enthroned at age 6, Mehmed IV was effectively ruled by a series of powerful grand viziers, including the Köprülü dynasty who revived Ottoman military power.

title: Leopold I becomes Emperor
description: Leopold I's long reign was defined by the Turkish wars and the War of the Grand Alliance, during which he successfully defended Vienna from the Ottoman siege.

title: Charles II restored as king
description: After eleven years of republican rule, the monarchy was restored when Charles II returned from exile, inaugurating the Restoration period.

title: Charles II becomes king
description: The last Habsburg king of Spain, Charles II was chronically ill and produced no heirs, making his death in 1700 the trigger for the War of the Spanish Succession.

title: Feodor III becomes Tsar
description: The sickly eldest son of Alexis I reigned only 6 years but abolished the hierarchical ranking system that had governed Russian social structure for generations.

title: James II becomes king
description: James II's open Catholicism and authoritarian style alarmed Protestant England, leading to the Glorious Revolution that replaced him within three years.

title: Suleiman II becomes Sultan
description: Suleiman II's reign coincided with catastrophic Ottoman defeats in Europe following the Second Siege of Vienna, as the Holy League pressed its advantage.

title: William III becomes king
description: The Dutch Prince of Orange was invited to rule England, ending Catholic Stuart rule and cementing constitutional monarchy via the Bill of Rights.

title: Ahmed II becomes Sultan
description: Ahmed II's brief reign was consumed by continued military defeats in Europe as the Holy League pressed its advantage after the relief of Vienna.

title: Mustafa II becomes Sultan
description: Mustafa II personally led military campaigns to recover lost Ottoman territories but was defeated at Zenta in 1697, leading to the humiliating Treaty of Karlowitz.

title: Battle of Flodden Field
description: English forces crushed the Scottish army, killing King James IV of Scotland in the bloodiest battle ever fought between the two kingdoms.

title: Battle of Chaldiran vs Safavid Persia
description: Selim I's decisive victory over Shah Ismail established the boundary between the Sunni Ottoman and Shia Safavid worlds that shapes the region to this day.

title: Thomas More publishes Utopia
description: More's Latin masterpiece imagined an ideal island society, coining the word 'utopia' and raising enduring questions about justice, wealth, and governance.

title: Ottomans conquer Egypt and Syria
description: Selim I's rapid conquest of the Mamluk sultanate gave the Ottomans control of the Arab heartland, Mecca, Medina, and the caliphate itself.

title: Magellan begins circumnavigation
description: Ferdinand Magellan led the first expedition to sail around the world, proving the Earth's roundness and the vastness of the Pacific Ocean.

title: Leonardo da Vinci dies in France
description: The Italian Renaissance master spent his final years under the patronage of Francis I, dying at the Château du Clos Lucé near Amboise.

title: Ottomans capture Belgrade
description: Suleiman's capture of this strategic Danubian fortress opened the road to Hungary and marked the beginning of Ottoman expansion deep into Central Europe.

title: Diet of Worms — Luther condemned
description: The Imperial council ordered Luther to recant his writings; when he refused, he was declared an outlaw, but was protected by the Elector of Saxony.

title: Magellan killed in the Philippines
description: Magellan was killed in the Battle of Mactan, but his fleet continued without him to complete history's first circumnavigation of the globe.

title: Ottomans capture Rhodes
description: The Knights of St. John were expelled from Rhodes after a six-month siege, removing the last Christian military presence in the eastern Mediterranean.

title: Battle of Mohács — Hungary crushed
description: The Ottoman army annihilated the Hungarian army in two hours, killing the king, and absorbing most of Hungary into the empire.

title: Charles V crowned Emperor by the Pope
description: The last Holy Roman Emperor to be crowned by a pope, Charles V's coronation in Bologna symbolized the peak of medieval Christian imperial power.

title: Pizarro conquers the Inca Empire
description: Francisco Pizarro and fewer than 200 men captured the Inca emperor Atahualpa, dismantling the world's largest empire at the time.

title: Michel de Montaigne born
description: The inventor of the personal essay, Montaigne's Essais explored the self with remarkable honesty, founding the modern literary form of the essay.

title: Henry VIII marries Anne Boleyn
description: Henry's secret marriage to Anne Boleyn — after his first marriage was annulled without papal consent — was the catalyst for the English break with Rome.

title: Dissolution of the Monasteries
description: Henry VIII seized and sold off England's monastic properties, transferring vast wealth to the crown and transforming the English countryside.

title: Battle of Preveza — Ottoman naval supremacy
description: Ottoman admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa's victory over a combined Christian fleet secured Ottoman control of the Mediterranean for the next three decades.

title: Society of Jesus (Jesuits) founded
description: Ignatius of Loyola founded the Jesuits in Rome to combat the Reformation through education and missionary work, creating the most influential Catholic teaching order.

title: Ottomans annex Hungary
description: The Ottoman Empire formally absorbed central Hungary as a province after years of contested control, turning it into a war zone for the next 150 years.

title: Copernicus publishes heliocentric model
description: De Revolutionibus claimed the Earth orbits the Sun — a revolutionary idea that transformed astronomy and humanity's sense of its place in the cosmos.

title: Council of Trent begins
description: The Catholic Church convened its great reform council to respond to the Protestant Reformation, defining Catholic doctrine for the next four centuries.

title: Silver discovered at Potosí
description: The discovery of the world's richest silver mountain in Bolivia flooded Europe with silver, fueling inflation and financing Spain's empire for 200 years.

title: Miguel de Cervantes born
description: Cervantes would write Don Quixote (1605), often called the first modern novel and one of the greatest works in world literature.

title: Ivan IV conquers Kazan
description: Ivan the Terrible's capture of the Tatar khanate of Kazan opened the door to Siberian expansion and marked Russia's first major conquest of a Muslim state.

title: Ivan IV conquers Astrakhan
description: Russia's conquest of Astrakhan gave it control of the Volga River from source to sea, opening overland trade routes to Persia and Central Asia.

title: Francis Bacon born
description: Bacon would become the father of the scientific method, arguing that knowledge must be built on observation and experiment rather than classical authority.

title: French Wars of Religion begin
description: A series of eight civil wars between French Catholics and Huguenots tore France apart for 36 years, killing hundreds of thousands.

title: Council of Trent concludes
description: After 18 years of sessions, the council clarified Catholic doctrine on every point Luther had challenged and launched the Counter-Reformation in earnest.

title: William Shakespeare born
description: The world's most celebrated playwright was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, eventually writing 37 plays that defined the English Renaissance theater.

title: Ivan IV's Oprichnina terror begins
description: Ivan the Terrible created a secret police state-within-a-state that conducted mass executions, confiscated noble lands, and terrorized Russia for seven years.

title: Dutch Revolt begins
description: The Protestant Netherlands began an 80-year war of independence against Catholic Spanish rule, eventually winning recognition of the Dutch Republic.

title: Johannes Kepler born
description: Kepler would discover the laws of planetary motion — that planets move in ellipses, not circles — transforming astronomy and supporting the Copernican model.

title: Sack of Antwerp
description: Mutinying Spanish soldiers sacked and burned Europe's wealthiest commercial city, killing 8,000 citizens — an atrocity that united the Dutch provinces against Spain.

title: Peter Paul Rubens born
description: The Flemish master would become Europe's most influential Baroque painter, renowned for large-scale mythological and religious canvases.

title: Ivan IV kills his own son in rage
description: Ivan struck his son and heir Tsarevich Ivan in a fit of rage, fatally wounding him and leaving the throne to his feeble second son Feodor — a dynastic catastrophe.

title: Gregorian calendar introduced
description: Pope Gregory XIII reformed the Julian calendar, dropping 10 days to correct centuries of drift — Catholic countries adopted it immediately, but Protestant ones resisted for over a century.

title: Mary Queen of Scots executed
description: After nearly 19 years imprisoned in England, Mary was beheaded at Elizabeth I's order, accused of plotting against the English crown.

title: René Descartes born
description: The 'father of modern philosophy,' Descartes would establish rationalism and analytic geometry, and coin the famous phrase 'I think, therefore I am.'

title: East India Company founded
description: A royal charter created the joint-stock trading company that would eventually control much of South Asia and define the British Empire.

title: Gunpowder Plot foiled
description: A group of Catholic conspirators including Guy Fawkes attempted to blow up Parliament and kill King James I but were exposed and executed.

title: Cervantes publishes Don Quixote
description: The tale of a deluded knight and his earthy squire is considered the first modern novel and one of the greatest works in all literature.

title: Time of Troubles begins
description: The death of Boris Godunov triggered a decade of civil war, foreign invasion, and competing pretenders that nearly destroyed the Russian state.

title: Jamestown colony founded
description: England's first permanent settlement in the Americas established a toehold on the Virginia coast that would grow into the thirteen colonies.

title: Champlain founds Quebec
description: Samuel de Champlain established New France's capital on the St. Lawrence River, making France the dominant colonial power in northern North America.

title: Kepler publishes laws of planetary motion
description: Kepler's first two laws — planets move in ellipses, sweeping equal areas in equal times — fundamentally revised the Copernican model and paved the way for Newton.

title: King James Bible published
description: Commissioned by King James I, this landmark English Bible shaped Protestant worship and the English language for centuries.

title: Shakespeare dies
description: The world's most famous playwright died at 52 in Stratford-upon-Avon, leaving behind a canon that continues to dominate world theater.

title: Cervantes dies
description: Miguel de Cervantes died in Madrid the same year as Shakespeare, and remarkably on the same date — April 23, 1616.

title: Battle of White Mountain
description: The crushing Catholic victory in Bohemia ended the brief Protestant experiment there and marked the beginning of Habsburg re-Catholicization across Central Europe.

title: Mayflower arrives in Plymouth
description: 102 Puritan separatists sailed from England and landed in Massachusetts, founding Plymouth Colony and signing the Mayflower Compact.

title: Richelieu becomes Chief Minister
description: Cardinal Richelieu restructured French government, crushed Protestant strongholds, and made France the dominant power in European politics during the Thirty Years' War.

title: Harvey describes blood circulation
description: William Harvey proved that blood circulates through the body pumped by the heart, overturning 1,400 years of Galenic medicine.

title: Siege of La Rochelle
description: Richelieu's 14-month siege of the Huguenot stronghold broke Protestant political power in France, centralizing royal authority at the cost of thousands of lives.

title: Johannes Kepler dies
description: Kepler died in poverty in Regensburg, his final years spent trying unsuccessfully to recover his mother from witchcraft accusations.

title: Battle of Breitenfeld
description: Sweden's decisive victory over the Catholic League — the first major Protestant victory in the Thirty Years' War — opened Germany to Swedish intervention.

title: John Locke born
description: Locke would become the philosopher of liberalism, arguing that governments derive legitimacy from the consent of the governed — ideas that shaped the American Revolution.

title: Descartes publishes Discourse on Method
description: Descartes' landmark work introduced his method of systematic doubt and the famous cogito ('I think, therefore I am'), founding modern rationalist philosophy.

title: Portugal breaks from Spain
description: After 60 years of union with Spain, Portugal successfully restored its independence under the House of Braganza in the Portuguese Restoration War.

title: English Civil War begins
description: Parliament's armed conflict against Charles I divided England into Royalists and Parliamentarians, resulting in the king's execution and a brief republican interlude.

title: Isaac Newton born
description: Newton would formulate the laws of motion and universal gravitation, invent calculus, and transform the scientific worldview of the Western world.

title: Battle of Rocroi — France defeats Spain
description: France's victory in this decisive battle ended Spanish military dominance in Europe and signaled the rise of France as the continent's leading power.

title: Hobbes publishes Leviathan
description: Thomas Hobbes argued that humans surrender rights to a powerful sovereign to escape the brutish 'state of nature' — a founding text of political philosophy.

title: First Anglo-Dutch War
description: England and the Dutch Republic clashed at sea over trade dominance, the first of three wars that would reshape Atlantic commerce.

title: Russia annexes Ukraine
description: The Pereyaslav Agreement brought the Cossack Hetmanate under Russian protection, beginning Russia's long westward expansion and its claim over Ukrainian lands.

title: Royal Society of London founded
description: The world's oldest national scientific institution was established to promote experimental knowledge, counting Newton, Boyle, and Wren among its early members.

title: England seizes New Amsterdam
description: English forces seized the Dutch colony at the tip of Manhattan and renamed it New York, cementing English dominance on the North American seaboard.

title: Great Plague of London
description: The last major outbreak of bubonic plague in England killed roughly 100,000 Londoners — about a quarter of the city's population — in 18 months.

title: Great Fire of London
description: A bakery fire in Pudding Lane spread to destroy 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and most of the medieval City of London over four days.

title: Ottomans conquer Crete
description: After a 24-year siege — the longest in history — the Ottomans captured the last major Venetian island, completing their control of the eastern Mediterranean.

title: France invades the Dutch Republic
description: Louis XIV's massive invasion humiliated the Dutch and triggered the coalitions that would define the rest of his reign.

title: Peter the Great born
description: Peter would transform Russia from a medieval kingdom into a European power, founding St. Petersburg and modernizing the army, navy, and government.

title: La Salle claims Louisiana for France
description: Explorer La Salle traveled the length of the Mississippi River and claimed the entire basin for France, naming it Louisiana after Louis XIV.

title: Edict of Nantes revoked
description: Louis XIV's revocation ended religious toleration for French Protestants, forcing 200,000 Huguenots to flee France and stripping the country of skilled craftsmen and merchants.

title: Newton publishes Principia Mathematica
description: Newton's masterwork laid out the laws of motion and universal gravitation, founding classical mechanics and the modern scientific worldview.

title: Peter the Great takes full power
description: After years of regency and power struggles, the 17-year-old Peter seized full control of Russia, ending the regency of his half-sister Sophia.

title: Locke publishes Essay on Human Understanding
description: Locke argued that the mind is a blank slate at birth and all knowledge comes from sensory experience, launching the Enlightenment tradition of empiricism.

title: Peter the Great tours Western Europe
description: Peter spent 18 months traveling incognito through Holland, England, and Austria, learning shipbuilding and recruiting experts to modernize Russia.

title: Treaty of Karlowitz — Ottoman losses
description: The treaty forced the Ottomans to cede Hungary, Transylvania, and Podolia to the Holy League — the greatest Ottoman territorial loss to that date.

title: Charles II dies — War of Spanish Succession begins
description: The death of Spain's last Habsburg king without heirs ignited a continent-wide war over who would inherit the Spanish Empire.

title: Great Northern War begins
description: Peter the Great's coalition against Sweden launched a war that would last 21 years and end with Russia emerging as the dominant power in the Baltic.

title: Cabral reaches Brazil
description: Pedro Álvares Cabral, blown west on his way to India, landed on the Brazilian coast and claimed it for Portugal — the beginning of Portuguese America.

title: Almeida becomes first Viceroy of India
description: Francisco de Almeida was appointed the first Viceroy of Portuguese India, anchoring Portugal's sea-borne empire in the Indian Ocean.

title: Portuguese capture Goa
description: Afonso de Albuquerque seized Goa, which became the capital of Portugal's eastern empire for the next four and a half centuries.

title: Capture of Malacca
description: Albuquerque took the great spice entrepôt of Malacca, giving Portugal control of the strait linking the Indian Ocean to the South China Sea.

title: Death of Manuel I
description: Manuel 'the Fortunate' died having presided over the golden age of Portuguese exploration — Vasco da Gama's India route, Brazil, and the spice trade.

title: Macau settlement established
description: Chinese authorities allowed Portuguese traders to settle at Macau, which became Europe's oldest permanent foothold in East Asia.

title: Camões publishes The Lusiads
description: Luís de Camões published Portugal's national epic, celebrating Vasco da Gama's voyage and the Portuguese age of discovery.

title: Battle of Alcácer Quibir
description: Young King Sebastian died invading Morocco along with much of the Portuguese nobility, triggering a succession crisis that ended in Spanish rule.

title: Iberian Union begins
description: Philip II of Spain claimed the vacant Portuguese throne, uniting the two crowns and their global empires for sixty years.

title: Loss of Hormuz
description: An Anglo-Persian force expelled the Portuguese from Hormuz after more than a century, marking the decline of their Indian Ocean monopoly.

title: Restoration — John IV crowned
description: A palace coup in Lisbon threw off Spanish rule and placed the Duke of Braganza on the throne as John IV, beginning the Restoration War.

title: Dutch expelled from Brazil
description: Portuguese and Brazilian forces retook Recife, ending a quarter century of Dutch rule in northeastern Brazil.

title: Spain recognizes Portuguese independence
description: The Treaty of Lisbon ended the Restoration War, with Spain formally recognizing the Braganza dynasty after 28 years of fighting.

title: Gold rush in Minas Gerais
description: Major gold strikes in the Brazilian interior set off the world's first great gold rush and bankrolled the Portuguese crown for decades.

title: Capture of Hormuz
description: Albuquerque took the island fortress of Hormuz, giving Portugal a chokehold on the Persian Gulf trade.

title: Dutch Revolt begins
description: The Battle of Heiligerlee opened the Eighty Years' War, the long Dutch struggle for independence from Habsburg Spain.

title: Sea Beggars take Brielle
description: Rebel privateers seized the port of Brielle, giving the revolt its first foothold on Dutch soil and sparking uprisings across Holland.

title: Union of Utrecht
description: The northern provinces bound themselves together in a defensive union that became the constitutional foundation of the Dutch Republic.

title: Act of Abjuration
description: The States General formally deposed Philip II — a landmark declaration that subjects could reject a king who ruled as a tyrant.

title: William of Orange assassinated
description: The father of the Dutch revolt was shot in Delft by a Catholic assassin — the first head of state killed with a handgun.

title: Dutch East India Company founded
description: The VOC, the world's first joint-stock megacorporation, was chartered with a monopoly on Asian trade and quasi-sovereign powers abroad.

title: Rembrandt born
description: Rembrandt van Rijn, the towering master of the Dutch Golden Age, was born in Leiden, the son of a miller.

title: Twelve Years' Truce
description: Spain and the Republic agreed to a long truce that amounted to de facto recognition of Dutch independence.

title: New Amsterdam founded
description: The Dutch West India Company planted a fortified trading town at the mouth of the Hudson — the future New York City.

title: Piet Hein captures the Spanish treasure fleet
description: Admiral Piet Hein seized the entire Spanish silver fleet in Matanzas Bay, Cuba — a haul so vast it funded the Dutch army for a year.

title: Tulip mania collapses
description: The speculative frenzy in tulip bulb contracts crashed spectacularly — often cited as the first recorded asset bubble.

title: Rembrandt completes The Night Watch
description: Rembrandt finished his monumental militia portrait, the most famous painting of the Dutch Golden Age.

title: Peace of Münster
description: Spain formally recognized the independence of the Dutch Republic, ending the Eighty Years' War as part of the Peace of Westphalia.

title: Rampjaar — the Disaster Year
description: France, England, and two German bishoprics attacked at once; the Republic survived by opening its dikes and flooding the land.

title: William III sails to England
description: Stadtholder William III landed at Torbay with a Dutch army and took the English throne in the Glorious Revolution.

title: Gustav Vasa elected king
description: Gustav Vasa's election ended the Kalmar Union with Denmark and founded the dynasty that built the modern Swedish state.

title: Swedish Reformation begins
description: The Diet of Västerås broke with Rome and transferred church lands to the crown, making Sweden an early Protestant kingdom.

title: Death of Gustav Vasa
description: The founder of independent Sweden died after 37 years on the throne, leaving a centralized kingdom to his quarrelsome sons.

title: Gustavus Adolphus born
description: The future 'Lion of the North', who would make Sweden a great power, was born in Stockholm.

title: Gustavus Adolphus becomes king
description: Sixteen-year-old Gustavus Adolphus inherited three wars and transformed Sweden's army into the most modern in Europe.

title: Treaty of Stolbovo
description: Russia ceded its Baltic coastline to Sweden, cutting Moscow off from the sea and cementing Swedish dominance of the region.

title: Warship Vasa sinks
description: The magnificently over-armed royal flagship Vasa capsized minutes into her maiden voyage in Stockholm harbor.

title: Sweden enters the Thirty Years' War
description: Gustavus Adolphus landed in Pomerania to champion the Protestant cause, turning the tide of the war in Germany.

title: Battle of Breitenfeld
description: Gustavus Adolphus crushed the Imperial army at Breitenfeld — the first major Protestant victory of the Thirty Years' War.

title: Gustavus Adolphus falls at Lützen
description: The warrior king was killed leading a cavalry charge in the fog at Lützen; his army won the battle but lost its soul.

title: Christina begins personal rule
description: Gustavus Adolphus's brilliant, unconventional daughter came of age and took the reins, turning her court into a haven for scholars.

title: Queen Christina abdicates
description: Christina stunned Europe by giving up her throne, converting to Catholicism, and moving to Rome.

title: Treaty of Roskilde
description: After a daring march across the frozen Danish straits, Sweden forced Denmark to cede Scania — its greatest territorial triumph.

title: Charles XII becomes king
description: The fourteen-year-old Charles XII inherited the Swedish empire; within three years, half of Europe would attack it.

title: Battle of Narva
description: Charles XII's tiny army routed a Russian force four times its size in a blizzard — a legendary opening to the Great Northern War.

title: Sigismund I becomes king
description: Sigismund the Old began a 42-year reign that brought the Italian Renaissance to the Polish court at Kraków.

title: The Prussian Homage
description: The last Teutonic Grand Master knelt to the Polish king and received Prussia as a secular duchy — the origin of the Prussian state.

title: Copernicus publishes heliocentric theory
description: On his deathbed in Frombork, Nicolaus Copernicus published De revolutionibus, placing the sun at the center of the cosmos.

title: Sigismund II Augustus crowned
description: The last Jagiellon king took the throne; his childlessness would push Poland and Lithuania into a permanent union.

title: Union of Lublin
description: Poland and Lithuania merged into a single Commonwealth — Europe's largest state and boldest experiment in elective monarchy.

title: Jagiellon dynasty ends
description: Sigismund II Augustus died without an heir, ending two centuries of Jagiellon rule; henceforth the nobility would elect its kings.

title: Warsaw Confederation
description: The Commonwealth's nobles guaranteed religious freedom by law — an extraordinary act of tolerance in an age of religious war.

title: Sigismund III elected king
description: The Swedish prince Sigismund Vasa won the Polish election, beginning a 45-year reign that entangled the Commonwealth in Sweden's wars.

title: Capital moved to Warsaw
description: Sigismund III shifted the royal court from Kraków to more centrally located Warsaw, which has been Poland's capital ever since.

title: Battle of Kircholm
description: Polish-Lithuanian winged hussars annihilated a Swedish army three times their number in one of history's most lopsided cavalry victories.

title: Polish troops occupy Moscow
description: After victory at Klushino, Commonwealth forces occupied the Kremlin for two years during Russia's Time of Troubles.

title: Khmelnytsky Uprising
description: A great Cossack revolt tore Ukraine away from the Commonwealth and began decades of catastrophic warfare.

title: The Deluge — Swedish invasion
description: Swedish armies overran nearly the whole Commonwealth in months; the devastation of the 'Deluge' rivaled the Thirty Years' War.

title: Sobieski relieves Vienna
description: John III Sobieski led the largest cavalry charge in history down the Kahlenberg, breaking the Ottoman siege of Vienna.

title: Death of John III Sobieski
description: The hero of Vienna died at his beloved Wilanów palace, the last great king of an increasingly paralyzed Commonwealth.

title: Zhengde becomes emperor
description: The pleasure-loving Zhengde Emperor took the Ming throne, leaving government increasingly to palace eunuchs.

title: Portuguese reach China
description: Jorge Álvares landed at the Pearl River delta — the first European to reach China by sea, opening direct East-West maritime trade.

title: Jiajing Emperor ascends
description: The Jiajing Emperor began a 45-year reign marked by Daoist obsessions, coastal piracy, and withdrawal from government.

title: Altan Khan raids Beijing
description: Mongol horsemen under Altan Khan burned the suburbs of Beijing itself, exposing the decay of Ming frontier defenses.

title: Ming lift the maritime trade ban
description: The Longqing Emperor legalized private overseas trade at Yuegang, drawing rivers of American silver into the Chinese economy.

title: Wanli era begins
description: The Wanli Emperor began the longest reign of the Ming dynasty — early brilliance under Grand Secretary Zhang Juzheng, then decades of imperial neglect.

title: Ming armies defend Korea
description: China sent massive armies to repel Hideyoshi's invasion of Korea — a victory that drained the Ming treasury for a generation.

title: Matteo Ricci received in Beijing
description: The Jesuit scholar Matteo Ricci was admitted to the imperial capital, beginning a remarkable exchange between European and Chinese learning.

title: Nurhaci founds the Later Jin
description: The Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci united the Manchu tribes and proclaimed a rival dynasty — the seed of the Qing conquest.

title: Chongzhen — last Ming emperor
description: The conscientious but doomed Chongzhen Emperor inherited an empire beset by famine, rebellion, and the Manchus.

title: Fall of the Ming dynasty
description: Rebels took Beijing and the Chongzhen Emperor hanged himself; within weeks the Manchus swept through the Great Wall and founded Qing rule.

title: Kangxi begins 61-year reign
description: The Kangxi Emperor began the longest reign in Chinese history, consolidating Qing rule and presiding over a golden age.

title: Koxinga takes Taiwan
description: The Ming loyalist Koxinga expelled the Dutch from Taiwan, establishing a base of resistance against the Qing.

title: Qing conquer Taiwan
description: Kangxi's fleet defeated the Koxinga regime at the Penghu islands, bringing Taiwan under mainland rule for the first time.

title: Treaty of Nerchinsk
description: China and Russia fixed their Siberian border in China's first treaty with a European power, negotiated partly in Latin by Jesuits.

title: Portuguese firearms reach Tanegashima
description: Shipwrecked Portuguese traders introduced the arquebus to Japan; within decades firearms transformed samurai warfare.

title: Francis Xavier arrives in Japan
description: The Jesuit missionary landed at Kagoshima, beginning Japan's 'Christian century' of missions and Nanban trade.

title: Battle of Okehazama
description: Oda Nobunaga's surprise attack destroyed a vastly larger army, launching his drive to unify war-torn Japan.

title: Nobunaga deposes the last Ashikaga shogun
description: Nobunaga drove shogun Ashikaga Yoshiaki from Kyoto, ending the 240-year Ashikaga shogunate.

title: Battle of Nagashino
description: Nobunaga's massed arquebusiers shattered the famed Takeda cavalry — a landmark in the military use of firearms.

title: Nobunaga killed at Honnō-ji
description: Betrayed by his general Akechi Mitsuhide, Nobunaga died in a burning Kyoto temple on the eve of unifying Japan.

title: Hideyoshi's sword hunt
description: Toyotomi Hideyoshi disarmed the peasantry nationwide, freezing the social order that would define Tokugawa Japan.

title: Hideyoshi invades Korea
description: Hideyoshi launched a massive invasion of Korea as a step toward conquering Ming China; the war ended in stalemate and retreat.

title: Death of Hideyoshi
description: The peasant-born unifier of Japan died leaving an infant heir — and a power vacuum Tokugawa Ieyasu would fill.

title: Battle of Sekigahara
description: Tokugawa Ieyasu's victory in a single foggy morning decided the mastery of Japan for the next 268 years.

title: Tokugawa shogunate founded
description: Ieyasu was appointed shogun and made his castle town of Edo — the future Tokyo — the true capital of Japan.

title: Fall of Osaka Castle
description: The Tokugawa destroyed the Toyotomi clan at Osaka, extinguishing the last serious challenge to their rule.

title: Shimabara Rebellion
description: A revolt of overtaxed peasants and persecuted Christians was crushed with terrible slaughter, hardening the ban on Christianity.

title: Sakoku — national seclusion
description: The shogunate expelled the Portuguese and sealed Japan to most of the world, a closure that lasted over two centuries.

title: Great Fire of Meireki
description: Fire consumed most of Edo and killed perhaps 100,000 people; the city was rebuilt into the world's largest metropolis.

title: First Battle of Panipat
description: Babur's cannon and cavalry destroyed the Delhi Sultanate's army, founding the Mughal Empire in a single day.

title: Death of Babur
description: The founder of the Mughal dynasty, poet and conqueror, died in Agra and was buried in his beloved Kabul.

title: Sher Shah drives out Humayun
description: The Afghan general Sher Shah Suri defeated Humayun at Kannauj, forcing the Mughal emperor into fifteen years of exile.

title: Humayun retakes Delhi
description: With Persian help, Humayun recovered his throne — only to die within a year after falling down his library stairs.

title: Akbar becomes emperor
description: Thirteen-year-old Akbar inherited a fragile kingdom and forged it into the greatest empire India had seen in centuries.

title: Akbar abolishes the jizya
description: Akbar repealed the tax on non-Muslims, a cornerstone of his policy of reconciliation with his Hindu subjects.

title: Fatehpur Sikri founded
description: Akbar built a magnificent new red-sandstone capital at Fatehpur Sikri — abandoned within decades for want of water.

title: Din-i Ilahi proclaimed
description: Akbar promulgated a syncretic court creed blending elements of India's faiths — emblematic of his religious experimentation.

title: Death of Akbar
description: Akbar the Great died at Agra, ruler of a hundred million subjects and architect of the Mughal state.

title: Jahangir marries Nur Jahan
description: The empress Nur Jahan became the real power behind Jahangir's throne — the most influential woman in Mughal history.

title: Shah Jahan crowned
description: Shah Jahan took the throne after a ruthless succession, beginning the golden age of Mughal architecture.

title: Taj Mahal construction begins
description: Grief-stricken at the death of Mumtaz Mahal, Shah Jahan began the white-marble mausoleum that became India's most famous monument.

title: Red Fort begun at Delhi
description: Shah Jahan founded his new capital of Shahjahanabad — Old Delhi — centered on the great Red Fort.

title: Aurangzeb seizes the throne
description: Aurangzeb imprisoned his father Shah Jahan and executed his brothers, winning the war of succession and ruling for half a century.

title: Shivaji crowned — the Maratha challenge
description: The Maratha leader Shivaji had himself crowned sovereign at Raigad, founding the power that would ultimately break Mughal dominance.

title: Ismail I founds the Safavid dynasty
description: Fourteen-year-old Ismail took Tabriz, proclaimed himself shah, and made Twelver Shi'ism the state religion — shaping Iran to this day.

title: Battle of Chaldiran
description: Ottoman cannon broke the Safavid cavalry at Chaldiran, fixing the long Ottoman-Persian rivalry and halting Safavid expansion west.

title: Death of Ismail I
description: The founder-shah died at 36, never having fully recovered from the shattering defeat at Chaldiran.

title: Peace of Amasya
description: Persia and the Ottomans signed their first formal peace, dividing the Caucasus and Mesopotamia between them.

title: Abbas the Great born
description: The future Abbas I, greatest of the Safavid shahs, was born in Herat amid a period of dynastic chaos.

title: Abbas the Great begins reign
description: Abbas I took the throne of a collapsing state and rebuilt it with a reformed army, new trade routes, and ruthless centralization.

title: Capital moved to Isfahan
description: Abbas made Isfahan his capital and rebuilt it so splendidly that Persians said 'Isfahan is half the world.'

title: Abbas retakes Tabriz
description: Abbas's modernized army recovered Tabriz and the lands lost to the Ottomans, avenging a century of defeats.

title: Naqsh-e Jahan Square completed
description: Isfahan's vast royal square — mosque, palace, and bazaar around one immense plaza — was completed as the showpiece of Safavid Persia.

title: Persia and England take Hormuz
description: Abbas allied with the English East India Company to expel the Portuguese from Hormuz, reopening the Gulf to Persian trade.

title: Death of Abbas the Great
description: Abbas died in Mazandaran at the height of Safavid power; none of his successors matched him.

title: Treaty of Zuhab
description: Persia and the Ottomans fixed a border along the Zagros that, in essence, still divides Iran and Iraq today.

title: Abbas II crowned
description: The last effective Safavid shah took the throne at age nine and later recovered Kandahar from the Mughals.

title: Suleiman I crowned
description: Suleiman's indolent reign began the long Safavid decline, as court factions and harem politics eclipsed government.

title: Sultan Husayn — the last Safavid century closes
description: The pious, pliable Sultan Husayn took the throne; within three decades the dynasty would fall to Afghan invaders.

title: Timbuktu flourishes under Askia rule
description: Under Askia Mohammad I, Timbuktu's Sankore mosque and its scholars made the city one of the great centers of Islamic learning.

title: Songhai campaigns into Hausaland
description: Askia Mohammad's armies pushed east against the Hausa city-states, carrying Songhai power toward its greatest extent.

title: Askia Mohammad deposed by his son
description: Old and blind, the great Askia was overthrown by his son Musa, opening decades of contested successions.

title: Death of Askia Mohammad I
description: The founder of the Askia dynasty died in Gao, remembered as one of the greatest rulers of West Africa.

title: Askia Daoud begins a prosperous reign
description: Askia Daoud's 33-year reign was Songhai's Indian summer — stability, trade, and patronage of Timbuktu's scholars.

title: Death of Askia Daoud
description: Daoud's death set off succession struggles that fatally weakened the empire on the eve of the Moroccan threat.

title: Morocco seizes the Taghaza salt mines
description: Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco took the Saharan salt mines that anchored Songhai's trade — the prelude to invasion.

title: Battle of Tondibi — fall of Songhai
description: A small Moroccan force armed with muskets crossed the Sahara and shattered the Songhai army, destroying West Africa's last great empire.

title: Timbuktu's scholars deported
description: The Moroccan occupiers arrested Timbuktu's leading scholars, including Ahmad Baba, and exiled them to Marrakesh — the end of an intellectual golden age.

title: Lebna Dengel crowned
description: The young emperor Dawit II, called Lebna Dengel, took the throne of the ancient Solomonic dynasty.

title: Portuguese embassy reaches Ethiopia
description: A Portuguese mission reached the court of Lebna Dengel, linking Christian Ethiopia with Europe after centuries of legend about 'Prester John'.

title: Ahmad Gragn's invasion begins
description: At Shimbra Kure, the forces of Ahmad ibn Ibrahim 'the Left-handed' crushed the imperial army, beginning a devastating fourteen-year war.

title: Cristóvão da Gama's expedition
description: Four hundred Portuguese musketeers under Vasco da Gama's son landed to aid Ethiopia; da Gama was captured and executed, but the tide turned.

title: Battle of Wayna Daga — Gragn defeated
description: Emperor Gelawdewos's army killed Ahmad Gragn near Lake Tana, ending the conquest that had nearly destroyed Christian Ethiopia.

title: Sarsa Dengel crowned
description: Sarsa Dengel began a long reign of consolidation, campaigning against Ottoman incursions and rebellious provinces alike.

title: Emperor Susenyos converts to Catholicism
description: Under Jesuit influence, Susenyos declared union with Rome — a decision that plunged the empire into civil war.

title: Fasilides restores the Orthodox faith
description: Fasilides took the throne, abandoned the union with Rome, and restored the ancient Ethiopian Orthodox church.

title: Jesuits expelled from Ethiopia
description: Fasilides expelled the Jesuit missionaries and closed Ethiopia to Europeans, beginning two centuries of deliberate isolation.

title: Fasilides founds Gondar
description: Fasilides established a permanent capital at Gondar, whose castles became the enduring symbol of imperial Ethiopia.

title: Moctezuma II crowned
description: Moctezuma II became huey tlatoani of the Aztec Empire at the height of its power, ruling millions from island Tenochtitlan.

title: The last New Fire ceremony
description: The Aztecs celebrated the binding of the years — the great calendar renewal held once every 52 years — for what proved to be the final time.

title: Cortés enters Tenochtitlan
description: Hernán Cortés marched into the Aztec capital and was received by Moctezuma; within months the emperor was a hostage in his own palace.

title: La Noche Triste
description: The Aztecs rose and drove the Spanish from Tenochtitlan with heavy losses — a victory that bought the empire one more year.

title: Smallpox devastates Tenochtitlan
description: Smallpox brought by the Europeans swept the Valley of Mexico, killing Emperor Cuitláhuac and untold thousands before the final siege.

title: Fall of Tenochtitlan
description: After a 93-day siege, Cuauhtémoc surrendered the ruined capital to Cortés — the end of the Aztec Empire and the birth of New Spain.

title: Execution of Cuauhtémoc
description: Cortés had the last Aztec emperor hanged during an expedition to Honduras, extinguishing the imperial line.

title: Huayna Capac expands the empire
description: Huayna Capac pushed the Inca realm to its greatest extent, from modern Colombia to Chile, ruling from Cusco and Quito.

title: Death of Huayna Capac
description: The emperor and his chosen heir died of a European epidemic that raced ahead of the conquistadors — setting off a catastrophic succession crisis.

title: Civil war: Huáscar against Atahualpa
description: The half-brothers fought a ruinous war for the throne; Atahualpa's victory came just as Pizarro's small band arrived on the coast.

title: Cajamarca — Atahualpa captured
description: Francisco Pizarro ambushed and seized Atahualpa at Cajamarca; the emperor's ransom filled a room with gold, but did not save him.

title: Execution of Atahualpa
description: The Spanish garroted the last independent Inca emperor despite his paid ransom, then marched on Cusco.

title: Fall of Cusco
description: Pizarro's men entered the Inca capital, stripping its temples of gold — the effective end of the empire, though resistance smoldered for decades.

title: Vilcabamba falls — the last Inca state ends
description: Spanish forces took the jungle refuge of Vilcabamba and executed Túpac Amaru, the final Inca of the neo-Inca state.

title: Act of Settlement secures Protestant succession
description: Parliament fixed the royal succession on the Protestant House of Hanover, shaping the British monarchy for centuries to come.

title: Acts of Union create Great Britain
description: England and Scotland united into a single kingdom with one parliament at Westminster, creating Great Britain.

title: George I begins the Hanoverian dynasty
description: The Elector of Hanover succeeded Queen Anne, bringing a German-speaking king to the British throne and cementing parliamentary government.

title: South Sea Bubble bursts
description: The collapse of wildly inflated South Sea Company stock ruined thousands of investors and became Britain's first great financial crash.

title: Death of Isaac Newton
description: The mathematician and physicist who formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation died in London and was buried in Westminster Abbey.

title: War of Jenkins' Ear begins
description: Britain declared war on Spain over trade in the Caribbean, a conflict named for a severed ear presented to Parliament.

title: Battle of Culloden
description: Government forces crushed the Jacobite rising of Bonnie Prince Charlie in the last pitched battle fought on British soil.

title: Britain adopts the Gregorian calendar
description: Britain finally switched from the Julian calendar, skipping eleven days so that 2 September was followed by 14 September.

title: Wolfe takes Quebec
description: General James Wolfe's victory on the Plains of Abraham delivered French Canada to Britain in the 'year of victories'.

title: Watt patents his improved steam engine
description: James Watt's separate condenser made steam power efficient, an invention that would drive the Industrial Revolution.

title: American colonies declare independence
description: Thirteen of Britain's American colonies declared themselves independent, beginning a war that would end with their loss in 1783.

title: First Fleet reaches Australia
description: Eleven ships carrying convicts and settlers founded the penal colony at Sydney Cove, beginning British settlement of Australia.

title: Jenner demonstrates smallpox vaccination
description: Edward Jenner showed that cowpox inoculation protected against smallpox, founding the science of vaccination.

title: Union with Ireland creates the United Kingdom
description: The Act of Union merged the Kingdom of Ireland with Great Britain, creating the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

title: Battle of Trafalgar
description: Nelson destroyed the combined French and Spanish fleets off Cape Trafalgar, dying in the moment of victory and securing British naval supremacy.

title: Battle of Waterloo
description: Wellington's allied army, with Prussian support, ended Napoleon's Hundred Days and a generation of war with France.

title: First public steam railway opens
description: The Stockton and Darlington Railway carried passengers behind Stephenson's Locomotion No. 1, opening the railway age.

title: Great Reform Act
description: Parliament redrew rotten boroughs and extended the franchise, beginning the long march toward British democracy.

title: Victoria becomes queen
description: The eighteen-year-old princess began a sixty-three-year reign that would give its name to an entire age.

title: Penny Post introduced
description: Rowland Hill's uniform penny postage and the Penny Black, the world's first adhesive stamp, revolutionized communication.

title: Irish Famine begins
description: Potato blight destroyed Ireland's staple crop; a million people died and a million more emigrated while Ireland remained under British rule.

title: Defeat at Blenheim
description: Marlborough and Prince Eugene shattered the Franco-Bavarian army on the Danube, ending Louis XIV's aura of invincibility.

title: Death of Louis XIV
description: The Sun King died at Versailles after seventy-two years on the throne, the longest reign in European history.

title: Mississippi Bubble collapses
description: John Law's paper-money scheme and Mississippi Company imploded, ruining investors and souring France on banks for decades.

title: War of the Polish Succession begins
description: France went to war with Austria over the Polish throne, eventually winning Lorraine for the French crown.

title: Montesquieu publishes The Spirit of the Laws
description: Montesquieu's treatise on the separation of powers became a founding text of modern constitutional government.

title: First volume of the Encyclopédie
description: Diderot and d'Alembert published the first volume of the Encyclopédie, the great collaborative project of the Enlightenment.

title: Treaty of Paris — France loses Canada
description: The Seven Years' War ended with France ceding Canada and lands east of the Mississippi to Britain, gutting its first colonial empire.

title: France allies with the American revolutionaries
description: The Franco-American alliance brought French ships, troops, and money to the American cause — and ruinous debt to the French crown.

title: First manned balloon flight
description: The Montgolfier brothers' hot-air balloon carried the first humans into the sky over Paris.

title: Storming of the Bastille
description: A Paris crowd seized the royal fortress-prison on 14 July, the symbolic beginning of the French Revolution.

title: First Republic proclaimed
description: The National Convention abolished the monarchy and declared France a republic amid war with the crowned heads of Europe.

title: Execution of Louis XVI
description: The king was guillotined in the Place de la Révolution, and the Terror soon followed.

title: Napoleon's coup of 18 Brumaire
description: General Bonaparte overthrew the Directory and made himself First Consul, ending the revolutionary decade.

title: Napoleon crowned emperor
description: Napoleon crowned himself Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame in the presence of the Pope.

title: Retreat from Moscow
description: Napoleon's Grande Armée of over 600,000 men was destroyed by winter, hunger, and Russian pursuit; barely a tenth returned.

title: Waterloo and the second abdication
description: Defeat at Waterloo ended Napoleon's Hundred Days; he abdicated again and was exiled to Saint Helena.

title: Death of Napoleon on Saint Helena
description: The exiled emperor died in British custody on the remote South Atlantic island of Saint Helena.

title: July Revolution
description: Three days of street fighting in Paris toppled Charles X and installed Louis Philippe as 'citizen king'.

title: Daguerreotype announced to the world
description: France presented Daguerre's photographic process as a gift 'free to the world', launching the age of photography.

title: February Revolution
description: Parisians overthrew the July Monarchy; the Second Republic that followed gave France universal male suffrage.

title: War of the Spanish Succession begins
description: Europe went to war over whether a Bourbon or a Habsburg would inherit the vast Spanish empire of the childless Charles II.

title: Treaty of Utrecht
description: The peace confirmed Philip V as king of Spain but stripped away Gibraltar, Menorca, and Spain's European possessions.

title: Siege of Gibraltar
description: Spain tried and failed to retake Gibraltar from Britain, a grievance that has outlasted every treaty since.

title: War of Jenkins' Ear
description: Spain fought Britain over Caribbean trade; the defense of Cartagena de Indias in 1741 became a celebrated Spanish victory.

title: Ferdinand VI becomes king
description: Ferdinand VI pursued peace and naval rebuilding, keeping Spain out of the Seven Years' War for most of his reign.

title: Charles III begins his reforms
description: The most effective of Spain's enlightened Bourbons modernized Madrid, the army, and colonial administration.

title: Jesuits expelled from Spain and its empire
description: Charles III expelled the Society of Jesus from all Spanish territories, seizing its schools and missions.

title: Great Siege of Gibraltar begins
description: Having joined the American war against Britain, Spain besieged Gibraltar for nearly four years without success.

title: Goya becomes court painter
description: Francisco de Goya rose to paint the royal family — and, in time, the darkest visions of Spain's wars and follies.

title: War of the Pyrenees
description: Spain joined the coalition against revolutionary France; two years of fighting ended in defeat and a forced alliance with Paris.

title: Trafalgar destroys the Spanish fleet
description: Fighting alongside France, Spain lost the heart of its navy off Cape Trafalgar, crippling its hold on a global empire.

title: Dos de Mayo uprising
description: Madrid rose against Napoleon's occupying troops; the brutal reprisals, painted by Goya, ignited the Peninsular War.

title: Constitution of Cádiz
description: Spain's first constitution, drafted while besieged by the French, became a liberal touchstone across Europe and Latin America.

title: Florida ceded to the United States
description: The Adams-Onís Treaty sold Florida to the United States as Spain struggled to hold its crumbling American empire.

title: Battle of Ayacucho ends the American empire
description: Defeat in the Peruvian highlands ended three centuries of Spanish rule on the South American mainland.

title: First Carlist War begins
description: The death of Ferdinand VII triggered civil war between supporters of the infant Isabella II and her absolutist uncle Don Carlos.

title: Guardia Civil founded
description: Spain created a national gendarmerie to police the countryside, an institution still in service today.

title: Prussia becomes a kingdom
description: Elector Frederick III crowned himself King Frederick I in Königsberg, elevating Brandenburg-Prussia within the Empire.

title: Charles VI elected emperor
description: The last male Habsburg emperor spent his reign securing recognition for his daughter Maria Theresa's inheritance.

title: Birth of Immanuel Kant
description: The philosopher who would redefine metaphysics, ethics, and aesthetics was born in Königsberg, which he almost never left.

title: Salzburg Protestants expelled
description: The Archbishop of Salzburg expelled some 20,000 Protestants, most of whom were resettled in Prussia.

title: Frederick the Great invades Silesia
description: Prussia's seizure of Habsburg Silesia opened decades of Austro-Prussian rivalry for mastery of Germany.

title: Seven Years' War begins
description: Frederick's preemptive strike into Saxony ignited a war that spread from central Europe to America and India.

title: Birth of Beethoven
description: Ludwig van Beethoven was born in Bonn; his music would bridge the classical and romantic ages.

title: Kant's Critique of Pure Reason
description: Kant's masterwork asked what reason can and cannot know, transforming philosophy from Königsberg outward.

title: Death of Mozart
description: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart died in Vienna at thirty-five, leaving his Requiem unfinished.

title: Holy Roman Empire dissolved
description: Under pressure from Napoleon, Francis II abdicated the imperial crown, ending an institution that had lasted a thousand years.

title: Battle of Leipzig
description: The 'Battle of the Nations' — the largest in Europe before 1914 — drove Napoleon out of Germany.

title: German Confederation founded
description: The Congress of Vienna bound thirty-nine German states into a loose confederation under Austrian presidency.

title: Death of Beethoven
description: Twenty thousand Viennese followed Beethoven's funeral procession; his nine symphonies had changed music forever.

title: Zollverein customs union
description: Prussia's customs union removed trade barriers among German states, knitting them together economically before politics followed.

title: March Revolution
description: Revolution swept the German states; the Frankfurt Parliament tried and failed to forge a unified, liberal Germany.

title: St Petersburg founded
description: Peter the Great founded a new capital on the Neva marshes, his 'window on Europe'.

title: Battle of Poltava
description: Peter's destruction of the Swedish army at Poltava broke Sweden's empire and announced Russia as a great power.

title: Naval victory at Gangut
description: Russia's first major naval victory, won against Sweden off the Hanko peninsula, established its Baltic fleet.

title: Russia becomes an empire
description: The Treaty of Nystad ended the Great Northern War; Peter took the title Emperor of All Russia.

title: Death of Peter the Great
description: The tsar who dragged Russia toward Europe died without naming an heir, opening an era of palace coups.

title: War with the Ottomans
description: Russia and Austria fought the Ottoman Empire for the Black Sea steppe; Russia retook Azov.

title: Bering reaches Alaska
description: Vitus Bering's second Kamchatka expedition sighted the Alaskan coast, opening Russian America to the fur trade.

title: Moscow University founded
description: Russia's first university was founded on the initiative of the polymath Mikhail Lomonosov.

title: Catherine the Great seizes power
description: A palace coup deposed Peter III in favor of his wife Catherine, who would rule Russia for thirty-four years.

title: Pugachev's Rebellion
description: A Cossack pretender claiming to be Peter III led the largest peasant revolt in Russian history across the Volga and Urals.

title: Annexation of Crimea
description: Catherine annexed the Crimean Khanate, giving Russia its long-sought warm-water foothold on the Black Sea.

title: Birth of Pushkin
description: Alexander Pushkin, the founder of modern Russian literature, was born in Moscow.

title: Alexander I becomes tsar
description: Alexander took the throne after the murder of his father Paul I in a palace conspiracy he had tacitly sanctioned.

title: Napoleon invades; Moscow burns
description: Russia absorbed Napoleon's invasion, gave up Moscow to fire, and destroyed the Grande Armée in the winter retreat.

title: Decembrist Revolt
description: Liberal officers rose against the new tsar Nicholas I in Senate Square; the revolt failed but its memory endured.

title: Pushkin killed in a duel
description: Russia's national poet died of wounds from a duel fought over his wife's honor, aged thirty-seven.

title: Dostoevsky's mock execution
description: Sentenced to death for radical politics, Dostoevsky was reprieved at the firing line and sent to Siberian katorga instead.

title: Ahmed III becomes sultan
description: A janissary revolt deposed Mustafa II and raised Ahmed III, whose reign would bloom into the Tulip Era.

title: Treaty of Passarowitz; Tulip Era begins
description: Peace with Austria and Venice opened a decade of palace refinement, poetry, and tulip cultivation in Istanbul.

title: First Ottoman printing press
description: İbrahim Müteferrika won permission to print books in Arabic script, founding the empire's first Muslim-run press.

title: Patrona Halil revolt
description: A janissary-led uprising ended the Tulip Era, deposed Ahmed III, and executed his grand vizier.

title: Treaty of Belgrade
description: The Ottomans recovered Belgrade from Austria, stabilizing the Danube frontier for a generation.

title: Peace with Persia
description: The Treaty of Kerden ended decades of war with Persia, fixing a border that has changed little since.

title: Mustafa III becomes sultan
description: Mustafa III attempted military and fiscal reform while storm clouds gathered on the Russian frontier.

title: War with Russia begins
description: The empire declared war on Catherine's Russia; six years of defeats would end in the disaster of Küçük Kaynarca.

title: Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca
description: A humiliating peace gave Russia Black Sea access and a claim to protect Orthodox Christians in Ottoman lands.

title: Selim III begins the New Order reforms
description: Selim III created a European-style army and new taxes to fund it — reforms that would cost him his throne and his life.

title: Napoleon invades Egypt
description: France seized Ottoman Egypt in a bid to cut Britain's route to India; the occupation collapsed within three years.

title: First Serbian Uprising
description: Serbian rebels rose against janissary misrule, beginning the long unraveling of Ottoman power in the Balkans.

title: Treaty of Bucharest
description: Peace with Russia ceded Bessarabia just weeks before Napoleon's invasion of Russia made the timing fateful.

title: Greek War of Independence begins
description: Greek revolutionaries rose against Ottoman rule; European intervention would secure Greek independence by 1832.

title: The Auspicious Incident
description: Mahmud II destroyed the janissary corps with artillery in Istanbul, clearing the way for a modern army.

title: Tanzimat reforms begin
description: The Rose Garden edict promised equal justice, secure property, and modern administration, opening the Tanzimat era.

title: London Straits Convention
description: The great powers agreed to close the Bosporus and Dardanelles to foreign warships in peacetime, propping up the empire.

title: Peace of Hubertusburg
description: The Seven Years' War ended in Germany with Prussia keeping Silesia, confirming its place among the great powers.

title: Great Exhibition at the Crystal Palace
description: Six million visitors saw the industrial wonders of the world under Paxton's glass palace in Hyde Park.

title: Darwin publishes On the Origin of Species
description: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection sold out its first printing in a day and remade the life sciences.

title: London Underground opens
description: The Metropolitan Railway carried 38,000 passengers on its first day — the world's first underground railway.

title: Victoria becomes Empress of India
description: Disraeli's Royal Titles Act crowned Victoria empress, symbolizing the Raj at the heart of the British Empire.

title: Victoria's Golden Jubilee
description: Fifty years of Victoria's reign were celebrated across an empire covering a quarter of the world's land.

title: Boer War begins
description: Britain's costly war against the Boer republics of South Africa dented imperial confidence and introduced the concentration camp to the world's vocabulary.

title: Death of Queen Victoria
description: Victoria died after nearly sixty-four years on the throne, closing the age that bore her name.

title: Titanic sinks
description: The 'unsinkable' White Star liner struck an iceberg on her maiden voyage; more than 1,500 people died.

title: Britain enters the First World War
description: Germany's invasion of Belgium brought Britain into the war; a generation died in the trenches of the Western Front.

title: Women gain the vote
description: The Representation of the People Act enfranchised women over thirty, the fruit of decades of suffragist struggle.

title: General Strike
description: 1.7 million workers struck in support of the miners; the nine-day strike ended in defeat for the unions.

title: Abdication crisis
description: Edward VIII gave up the throne to marry Wallis Simpson, and his brother became George VI.

title: Battle of Britain
description: RAF Fighter Command defeated the Luftwaffe over southern England, ending the threat of German invasion.

title: National Health Service founded
description: Aneurin Bevan's NHS made healthcare free at the point of use, the centerpiece of the postwar welfare state.

title: Suez Crisis
description: The Anglo-French invasion of Egypt collapsed under American pressure, exposing the end of Britain's imperial reach.

title: England wins the World Cup
description: England beat West Germany 4–2 at Wembley, the country's only football World Cup triumph.

title: Britain joins the EEC
description: After two French vetoes, Britain entered the European Economic Community under Edward Heath.

title: Falklands War
description: Britain retook the Falkland Islands from Argentina in a ten-week war fought 8,000 miles from home.

title: Channel Tunnel opens
description: Britain and France were joined by rail beneath the Channel, realizing a dream first proposed in 1802.

title: Handover of Hong Kong
description: Britain returned Hong Kong to China, an event widely seen as the final act of the British Empire.

title: Second Empire proclaimed
description: A year after his coup, President Louis-Napoléon became Emperor Napoleon III.

title: Hugo publishes Les Misérables
description: Victor Hugo's epic of poverty and redemption appeared while its author lived in political exile from Napoleon III.

title: Franco-Prussian War; Third Republic proclaimed
description: Defeat at Sedan toppled Napoleon III; Paris proclaimed a republic even as Prussian armies closed in.

title: Eiffel Tower completed
description: Built for the centennial Exposition Universelle, the iron tower was reviled by artists and adored by everyone else.

title: Dreyfus Affair begins
description: The wrongful treason conviction of a Jewish officer split France for a decade and gave the world Zola's 'J'Accuse...!'.

title: First Tour de France
description: Sixty riders set off from Paris on a 2,428-kilometer race conceived to sell newspapers; a national institution was born.

title: First Battle of the Marne
description: French and British forces stopped the German advance on Paris; taxis ferried reserves to the front.

title: Battle of Verdun
description: Ten months of attrition at Verdun cost some 700,000 casualties and became France's defining ordeal of the war.

title: Paris Olympics
description: The last Games organized under Pierre de Coubertin featured the runners later immortalized in Chariots of Fire.

title: Popular Front wins power
description: Léon Blum's left-wing coalition brought the forty-hour week and paid holidays to French workers.

title: Fall of France
description: German armies defeated France in six weeks; Pétain signed an armistice while de Gaulle called for resistance from London.

title: Liberation of Paris
description: French and American forces freed the capital; de Gaulle walked down the Champs-Élysées the next day.

title: Fall of Dien Bien Phu
description: The Viet Minh's victory ended French Indochina and foreshadowed the wars of decolonization to come.

title: Fifth Republic founded
description: The Algerian crisis returned de Gaulle to power with a new constitution built around a strong presidency.

title: Algerian independence
description: The Évian Accords ended eight years of war and 132 years of French rule in Algeria.

title: May 1968 protests
description: Student revolt and a general strike of ten million workers brought France to a standstill and shook de Gaulle's republic.

title: Concorde enters service
description: The Anglo-French supersonic airliner began scheduled flights, crossing the Atlantic in under four hours.

title: Mitterrand elected; death penalty abolished
description: France's first Socialist president of the Fifth Republic abolished the guillotine in his first year.

title: France wins the World Cup
description: Zidane's two headers beat Brazil in Saint-Denis, and a multiracial team became a symbol of modern France.

title: War with Morocco
description: Spain's campaign in northern Morocco stirred patriotic fervor and expanded its enclaves around Ceuta.

title: Glorious Revolution deposes Isabella II
description: A military rising drove the queen into French exile and opened six turbulent years of experiments in government.

title: First Republic proclaimed
description: After King Amadeo abdicated in despair, the Cortes proclaimed a republic that burned through four presidents in eleven months.

title: Barcelona Universal Exposition
description: Barcelona's world fair announced the Catalan capital's industrial confidence and modernista architecture.

title: Spanish-American War — the empire lost
description: Defeat by the United States cost Spain Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines, a national trauma known simply as 'the Disaster'.

title: Tragic Week in Barcelona
description: Anticonscription riots and church burnings in Barcelona were harshly suppressed, deepening Spain's social divides.

title: Primo de Rivera's coup
description: General Miguel Primo de Rivera seized power with the king's blessing, ruling as dictator until 1930.

title: Second Republic proclaimed
description: Municipal elections swept away the monarchy; Alfonso XIII left Spain without formally abdicating.

title: Civil War begins
description: A military rising against the Republic split Spain in two and drew in fascist and Soviet arms, volunteers, and ideologies.

title: Bombing of Guernica
description: The German Condor Legion destroyed the Basque town of Guernica; Picasso's mural made it the century's emblem of terror from the air.

title: Franco victorious
description: Madrid fell and the Republic collapsed; Franco's dictatorship would last thirty-six years.

title: Spain declared a kingdom in waiting
description: Franco's Law of Succession made Spain nominally a monarchy again — with the throne vacant and himself as regent for life.
