title: Pact of Madrid with the United States
description: American bases in exchange for aid ended Spain's postwar isolation and propped up the Franco regime.

title: Juan Carlos named successor
description: Franco designated the young Bourbon prince as his successor, expecting continuity; Juan Carlos would deliver democracy instead.

title: Death of Franco
description: The dictator died in his bed after months of agony; within three years Spain had a democratic constitution.

title: Democratic constitution approved
description: Spaniards overwhelmingly ratified a constitution establishing parliamentary monarchy and regional autonomy.

title: Spain joins the European Community
description: EC membership sealed Spain's return to the European mainstream after decades of dictatorship.

title: Barcelona Olympics
description: The Games remade Barcelona's waterfront and showcased a confident, democratic Spain to the world.

title: Neanderthal fossil discovered
description: Quarry workers in the Neander valley near Düsseldorf unearthed bones of an ancient human species that now bears the valley's name.

title: Bismarck becomes minister-president of Prussia
description: Appointed to break a budget deadlock, Bismarck promised to settle the great questions by 'iron and blood'.

title: Austro-Prussian War
description: Prussia's seven-week victory over Austria at Königgrätz expelled Vienna from German affairs and dissolved the Confederation.

title: German Empire proclaimed at Versailles
description: After victory over France, the German princes proclaimed Wilhelm I emperor in the Hall of Mirrors.

title: Bismarck's health insurance law
description: The world's first national health insurance system was built to undercut socialism — and became a model for the welfare state.

title: Röntgen discovers X-rays
description: Wilhelm Röntgen's mysterious rays, discovered at Würzburg, won the first Nobel Prize in Physics.

title: First Zeppelin flight
description: Count Zeppelin's rigid airship LZ 1 rose over Lake Constance, beginning Germany's love affair with the airship.

title: Germany enters the First World War
description: The Schlieffen Plan sent German armies through Belgium toward Paris — and into four years of two-front war.

title: Kaiser abdicates; republic declared
description: Defeat and revolution ended the Hohenzollern monarchy; Wilhelm II fled to the Netherlands.

title: Hyperinflation
description: The mark collapsed to trillions per dollar; wheelbarrows of banknotes became the enduring image of Weimar's crisis year.

title: Hitler appointed chancellor
description: President Hindenburg appointed Hitler on 30 January; within months the Nazis had destroyed German democracy.

title: Kristallnacht
description: Nazi mobs burned synagogues and smashed Jewish shops across Germany in a state-orchestrated pogrom.

title: Surrender and division
description: Germany surrendered unconditionally in May 1945 and was divided into four occupation zones by the victorious Allies.

title: Berlin Airlift
description: When the Soviets blockaded West Berlin, Allied aircraft supplied two million people for eleven months.

title: West Germany joins NATO
description: Ten years after the war, the Federal Republic rearmed within the Western alliance; the Warsaw Pact formed in response.

title: Berlin Wall built
description: East Germany sealed off West Berlin overnight to stop the flood of emigration, dividing the city for twenty-eight years.

title: Munich Olympics
description: West Germany's 'cheerful Games' were shattered by the terrorist murder of eleven Israeli athletes.

title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
description: A bungled press conference opened the border on 9 November; Berliners danced on the Wall as the Cold War order dissolved.

title: German reunification
description: East Germany acceded to the Federal Republic on 3 October, restoring a single German state after forty-five years.

title: Crimean War begins
description: Russia's war with the Ottomans drew in Britain and France; defeat at home exposed the empire's backwardness.

title: Emancipation of the serfs
description: Alexander II freed more than twenty million serfs, the greatest single act of emancipation in European history.

title: Tolstoy completes War and Peace
description: Tolstoy's epic of the Napoleonic invasion appeared in full, a summit of the Russian novel.

title: Alexander II assassinated
description: A People's Will bomb killed the tsar-liberator on a St Petersburg street; repression followed reform.

title: Trans-Siberian Railway begun
description: Russia began the world's longest railway, nearly 6,000 miles from Moscow to Vladivostok.

title: Revolution of 1905
description: Bloody Sunday, naval mutiny, and a general strike forced Nicholas II to concede Russia's first parliament.

title: October Revolution
description: Lenin's Bolsheviks seized Petrograd's key points and the Winter Palace, taking Russia out of the war and into revolution.

title: Death of Lenin
description: Lenin died at fifty-three; his embalmed body went on display as Stalin outmaneuvered rivals for succession.

title: Famine amid collectivization
description: Forced collectivization of agriculture brought catastrophic famine to Ukraine, Kazakhstan, and southern Russia.

title: Germany invades the Soviet Union
description: Operation Barbarossa opened the largest land war in history; the Soviet Union would lose some 27 million people.

title: Fall of Berlin
description: The Red Army took the German capital at a cost of 80,000 dead and raised the Soviet flag over the Reichstag.

title: Sputnik launched
description: The first artificial satellite circled the Earth every 96 minutes, stunning the West and opening the space age.

title: Gagarin orbits the Earth
description: Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space, completing one orbit in 108 minutes aboard Vostok 1.

title: Invasion of Afghanistan
description: Soviet troops entered Afghanistan to prop up a communist government, beginning a nine-year war often called the USSR's Vietnam.

title: Chernobyl disaster
description: Reactor No. 4 exploded in the world's worst nuclear accident; the cover-up corroded faith in the Soviet system.

title: Dissolution of the Soviet Union
description: After a failed coup and the secession of its republics, the USSR ceased to exist on 26 December 1991.

title: Crimean War
description: With British and French help, the empire fought Russia to a standstill — and was admitted, briefly, to the concert of Europe.

title: Suez Canal opens
description: The canal through nominally Ottoman Egypt joined the Mediterranean to the Red Sea and rewired world trade.

title: First Ottoman constitution
description: Sultan Abdulhamid II granted a constitution and parliament — then suspended both within two years.

title: Birth of Mustafa Kemal
description: The future Atatürk was born in Ottoman Salonika; he would dismantle the empire that raised him and build a republic.

title: Greco-Turkish War
description: The empire won a quick war with Greece over Crete — its last clear military victory in Europe.

title: Young Turk Revolution
description: Army officers forced the sultan to restore the constitution, bringing the Committee of Union and Progress to power.

title: Gallipoli campaign
description: Ottoman forces repelled the Allied landings at Gallipoli; the defense made Mustafa Kemal's name.

title: Republic of Turkey proclaimed
description: After victory in the War of Independence, Mustafa Kemal proclaimed a republic with its capital at Ankara.

title: Alphabet reform
description: Turkey replaced Arabic script with a Latin alphabet in months, part of Atatürk's sweeping secular reforms.

title: Death of Atatürk
description: The founder of modern Turkey died at Dolmabahçe Palace; clocks there are still stopped at 9:05.

title: Turkey joins the United Nations
description: Having stayed neutral for most of the war, Turkey declared war on Germany in February 1945 and became a UN founding member.

title: First free elections
description: The Democrat Party's landslide ended a quarter-century of one-party rule, Turkey's first peaceful transfer of power.

title: Turkey joins NATO
description: Turkey entered the Atlantic alliance alongside Greece, anchoring NATO's southeastern flank.

title: Military coup
description: The army overthrew the Menderes government; the prime minister was executed the following year.

title: Cyprus intervention
description: Turkey landed troops on Cyprus after a Greek-backed coup, dividing the island along a line that still stands.

title: Military coup of 1980
description: General Evren's coup ended years of political violence at the cost of mass arrests and a new authoritarian constitution.

title: İzmit earthquake
description: A magnitude 7.6 earthquake near İzmit killed more than 17,000 people and reshaped Turkish politics and building codes.

title: Prime Minister Canalejas assassinated
description: An anarchist shot the reforming liberal prime minister José Canalejas outside a Madrid bookshop.

title: Russo-Turkish War
description: Russia's victory in the Balkans freed Bulgaria from Ottoman rule and brought its armies within sight of Istanbul.

title: Methuen Treaty with England
description: The treaty traded Portuguese wine for English cloth, binding Portugal's economy and diplomacy to England for a century.

title: Portuguese fleet victorious at Matapan
description: John V's navy helped Venice and the Pope defeat the Ottomans off Cape Matapan, a showpiece of Portugal's gold-funded prestige.

title: Mafra Palace consecrated
description: The colossal palace-convent of Mafra, paid for with Brazilian gold, embodied John V's ambition to rival Rome and Versailles.

title: Águas Livres Aqueduct completed
description: Lisbon's great aqueduct began carrying water across the Alcântara valley; its arches survived the earthquake seven years later.

title: Great Lisbon earthquake
description: Earthquake, tsunami, and fire destroyed Lisbon on All Saints' Day, killing tens of thousands and shaking Europe's faith in providence.

title: Pombal expels the Jesuits
description: The Marquis of Pombal expelled the Society of Jesus from Portugal and its empire, the first of Europe's crowns to do so.

title: Slavery abolished in mainland Portugal
description: Pombal banned the import of slaves to metropolitan Portugal — while the trade continued to Brazil.

title: Maria I crowned; Pombal falls
description: Portugal's first queen regnant dismissed the all-powerful Pombal and reversed much of his authoritarian reform program.

title: Prince John assumes the regency
description: With Maria I declared incurably mad, her son John governed in her name through the storms of the revolutionary era.

title: Royal court flees to Brazil
description: Ahead of Junot's invading army, the entire Portuguese court sailed for Rio de Janeiro under British escort — a capital moved across an ocean.

title: French driven from Portugal
description: Wellington's lines of Torres Vedras starved Masséna's army out of Portugal, ending the third and last French invasion.

title: Brazil declares independence
description: Prince Pedro's 'Independence or Death!' cry on the Ipiranga cost Portugal its vast American colony and half its revenue.

title: Miguelite War begins
description: Miguel I seized absolute power from his niece Maria II, plunging Portugal into six years of civil war between absolutists and liberals.

title: Liberals win; Miguel exiled
description: The Concession of Evoramonte ended the civil war, restored Maria II, and banished Miguel from Portugal for life.

title: Maria da Fonte uprising
description: A popular revolt that began among the women of the Minho spiraled into a brief civil war, settled only by British and Spanish intervention.

title: William III dies; stadtholderless again
description: The king-stadtholder's death without heirs opened the Second Stadtholderless Period, with the Republic run by the regent oligarchy.

title: Peace of Utrecht signed at home
description: The treaties ending the War of the Spanish Succession were signed in Utrecht — a diplomatic triumph hosted by an exhausted Republic.

title: Bubble year touches Amsterdam
description: Speculative manias in Paris and London spilled into Dutch markets; Amsterdam's exchange weathered the crash better than most.

title: Shipworm plague threatens the dikes
description: The teredo worm was found devouring the wooden dike revetments, forcing a ruinous rebuilding of the sea defenses in stone.

title: Orangist revolution restores the stadtholderate
description: French invasion panic swept William IV to power as hereditary stadtholder of all seven provinces — the first ever.

title: Infant William V inherits the stadtholderate
description: William IV's death left a three-year-old heir; decades of regency deepened the Republic's political drift.

title: Amsterdam banking crisis
description: The collapse of the De Neufville house after the Seven Years' War rippled through Europe — an early international financial contagion.

title: Eisinga begins his planetarium
description: Frisian wool-comber Eise Eisinga started building a working orrery into his living-room ceiling; it remains the world's oldest functioning planetarium.

title: Fourth Anglo-Dutch War
description: Britain declared war over Dutch trade with the American rebels; defeat shattered the Republic's commerce and prestige.

title: Prussian army crushes the Patriots
description: A Prussian invasion restored William V against the democratic Patriot movement; thousands of Patriots fled to France.

title: Batavian Revolution
description: French armies crossed the frozen rivers and the old Republic dissolved into the Batavian Republic; William V fled to England.

title: Louis Bonaparte made King of Holland
description: Napoleon installed his brother as king; Louis took his new country's side often enough to be removed within four years.

title: Kingdom of the Netherlands proclaimed
description: The Congress of Vienna united the northern and southern Netherlands under William I as a buffer against France.

title: Belgium secedes
description: Revolution in Brussels split the kingdom in two; William I refused to accept the loss for nine expensive years.

title: Thorbecke's liberal constitution
description: Frightened by revolutions abroad, William II accepted a constitution that made ministers answerable to parliament, founding Dutch parliamentary democracy.

title: Disaster at Poltava
description: Charles XII's army was destroyed deep in Ukraine; the king fled to Ottoman territory and Sweden's Baltic empire began to unravel.

title: Charles XII shot at Fredriksten
description: The warrior king was killed by a projectile in the trenches before a Norwegian fortress, ending two decades of ceaseless campaigning.

title: Peace of Nystad ends the empire
description: Sweden ceded its eastern Baltic provinces to Russia, closing the Great Northern War and its own great-power age.

title: The Hats take power
description: In the parliamentary Age of Liberty, the war-minded Hat party ousted the cautious Caps — party politics a half-century before the French Revolution.

title: Celsius proposes his temperature scale
description: Uppsala astronomer Anders Celsius defined a hundred-degree scale between water's boiling and freezing points.

title: Linnaeus publishes Species Plantarum
description: Carl Linnaeus catalogued every plant known to him under two-word Latin names, founding modern biological nomenclature.

title: World's first freedom of the press act
description: Sweden's Riksdag abolished censorship and opened official documents to the public — the world's first such law.

title: Gustav III's bloodless coup
description: The young king surrounded the Riksdag with loyal troops and ended the Age of Liberty, restoring royal power by charm and bayonet.

title: Swedish Academy founded
description: Gustav III founded the Academy of eighteen chairs 'for the purity, strength and sublimity of the Swedish language'; it would later award the Nobel Prize in Literature.

title: Gustav III assassinated at the opera
description: A masked conspirator shot the king at a midnight masquerade in his own opera house; he died two weeks later.

title: Finland lost to Russia
description: Defeat in the Finnish War cost Sweden a third of its territory and triggered a coup, a new constitution, and a new dynasty.

title: Union with Norway
description: The Treaty of Kiel and a short campaign brought Norway into personal union under the Swedish crown — Sweden's last war.

title: Berzelius isolates silicon
description: Jöns Jacob Berzelius, the great systematizer of chemistry, first isolated the element that would one day name an age.

title: Göta Canal completed
description: After 22 years of digging, the canal linked the Baltic to the North Sea across Sweden's heart — the largest civil engineering work in its history.

title: Compulsory schooling introduced
description: Every parish was ordered to open an elementary school, an early foundation of the country's later social model.

title: Augustus II restored after Poltava
description: Russia's victory over Sweden returned Augustus the Strong to the Polish throne — and made the Commonwealth a Russian protectorate in all but name.

title: The Silent Sejm
description: Under Russian guns, a one-day parliament passed sweeping limits on the army and the king without a single deputy being allowed to speak.

title: Tumult of Thorn
description: A religious riot in Toruń ended with Protestant leaders executed, a scandal that Europe's powers used to paint the Commonwealth as intolerant.

title: War of the Polish Succession
description: Rival elections of Stanisław Leszczyński and Augustus III dragged Europe into war; Russian arms decided who would be king of Poland.

title: Załuski Library opens
description: The brothers Załuski opened one of Europe's first great public libraries in Warsaw; Russia would cart it off after the partitions.

title: Foreign armies cross a neutral Commonwealth
description: During the Seven Years' War, Russian and Prussian armies marched across Poland at will — neutrality without the power to enforce it.

title: Stanisław II Augustus elected
description: Catherine the Great's former favorite was elected king with Russian backing; he would be the Commonwealth's last monarch and its greatest patron of the arts.

title: First Partition
description: Russia, Prussia, and Austria each carved off a slice of the Commonwealth, taking a third of its land and people at the stroke of a pen.

title: Constitution of 3 May
description: Europe's first modern written constitution abolished the liberum veto and created a constitutional monarchy — too late to save the state that wrote it.

title: Kościuszko Uprising
description: The hero of two continents led a national rising with scythe-armed peasants in its ranks; its defeat sealed the Commonwealth's fate.

title: Third Partition erases the Commonwealth
description: The three partitioning powers divided what remained; after eight centuries, Poland vanished from the map of Europe.

title: Chopin born at Żelazowa Wola
description: Frédéric Chopin was born in a manor house west of Warsaw; his music would carry Poland's voice through a century without a Polish state.

title: November Uprising
description: Cadets in Warsaw rose against Russian rule; a year of full-scale war ended in defeat, repression, and the Great Emigration.

title: Kraków Uprising
description: A rising in the free city of Kraków was crushed within days; Austria annexed the last unpartitioned scrap of old Poland.

title: Royal Academy of History founded
description: John V founded the Academia Real da História to document Portugal's past, part of his campaign of gold-funded cultural prestige.

title: Estrela Basilica consecrated
description: Maria I's great domed basilica, built in thanks for the birth of an heir, rose over Lisbon as the earthquake city rebuilt itself.

title: Anglo-Dutch Treaty divides the East
description: Britain and the Netherlands drew a line through the Malay world, trading Singapore's hinterland for Sumatra and shaping two future nations.

title: The Great Sejm convenes
description: The four-year reform parliament set out to rescue the Commonwealth, doubling the army and drafting the constitution of 1791.

title: Napoleon creates the Duchy of Warsaw
description: From Prussia's partition spoils Napoleon carved a small Polish state — hope enough to send Polish legions across Europe under his eagles.

title: Mickiewicz launches Polish Romanticism
description: Adam Mickiewicz's Ballads and Romances opened the Romantic age in Polish letters — a literature that kept the nation alive without a state.

title: First Portuguese railway opens
description: The line from Lisbon to Carregado carried Portugal's first train, the start of the Regeneration era's public works.

title: Typhoid kills the young king
description: Pedro V and two of his brothers died of typhoid within weeks, a blow from which the Braganza monarchy's prestige never fully recovered.

title: Eça de Queirós publishes Cousin Bazilio
description: The great realist skewered Lisbon's bourgeoisie; his novels remain the defining portrait of nineteenth-century Portugal.

title: The British Ultimatum
description: Britain forced Portugal to abandon its claim to the land between Angola and Mozambique; the humiliation fed republican sentiment at home.

title: The Lisbon Regicide
description: Carlos I and the crown prince were shot dead in an open carriage on the Terreiro do Paço; the monarchy outlived them by two years.

title: Republic proclaimed
description: A two-day revolution in Lisbon deposed Manuel II and proclaimed the Portuguese Republic, ending nearly eight centuries of monarchy.

title: Portugal enters the First World War
description: Germany declared war after Portugal seized German ships at British request; a Portuguese corps would suffer terribly in Flanders.

title: Military coup ends the First Republic
description: After sixteen chaotic years and forty-five governments, the army seized power — the dictatorship it installed would last until 1974.

title: Salazar becomes prime minister
description: The Coimbra economics professor took full power and built the Estado Novo, Europe's most durable authoritarian regime.

title: Neutral Lisbon, city of refugees
description: While the regime staged the Exposition of the Portuguese World, neutral Lisbon became wartime Europe's escape hatch to the Atlantic.

title: Portugal joins the United Nations
description: Admission ended postwar isolation, even as the regime insisted its African colonies were mere 'overseas provinces'.

title: Colonial war begins in Angola
description: Uprisings in Angola opened thirteen years of war across three African territories that would consume half the state budget and the regime itself.

title: Carnation Revolution
description: Junior officers overthrew the Estado Novo almost without bloodshed; Lisbon crowds put carnations in the soldiers' rifle barrels.

title: The African colonies go free
description: Angola, Mozambique, and the other territories became independent within eighteen months, ending Europe's oldest colonial empire.

title: Portugal joins the European Community
description: Entry alongside Spain anchored the young democracy in Europe and opened a decade of rapid modernization.

title: Expo '98 in Lisbon
description: The last world's fair of the century remade Lisbon's eastern waterfront around the theme of the oceans, five hundred years after Vasco da Gama.

title: Parliamentary government takes root
description: Under Thorbecke's constitution the Netherlands settled into ministerial responsibility and party government.

title: Slavery abolished in the colonies
description: The Netherlands freed the enslaved of Suriname and the Antilles — with owners compensated and the freed bound to ten more years of contract labor.

title: North Sea Canal opens
description: A canal cut straight through the dunes gave Amsterdam a modern route to the sea and revived its port.

title: Rijksmuseum opens
description: Cuypers' cathedral-like museum opened in Amsterdam with Rembrandt's Night Watch at its heart.

title: Death of Vincent van Gogh
description: The painter died nearly unknown at Auvers-sur-Oise; within a generation he was among the most famous artists who ever lived.

title: First Nobel laureate in chemistry
description: Jacobus van 't Hoff won the first Nobel Prize in Chemistry, opening a golden age of Dutch science.

title: Neutral in the Great War
description: The Netherlands stayed out of the First World War, absorbing a million Belgian refugees while the armies dug in next door.

title: Amsterdam Olympics
description: The Games introduced the Olympic flame; a Dutch crowd watched women run track events for the first time.

title: Afsluitdijk closes the Zuiderzee
description: A 32-kilometer dam turned an arm of the sea into a freshwater lake, the boldest stroke in the Dutch war against water.

title: German invasion; Rotterdam bombed
description: Five days of war ended when the Luftwaffe leveled central Rotterdam; the queen and government fled to London.

title: The Hunger Winter
description: A German blockade of the occupied west brought famine; more than 20,000 Dutch civilians starved before liberation.

title: Indonesian independence recognized
description: After four years of war and international pressure, the Netherlands transferred sovereignty over the East Indies to Indonesia.

title: North Sea flood
description: A storm surge broke the dikes of Zeeland by night, killing more than 1,800 people and launching the Delta Works.

title: Founding member of the EEC
description: The Netherlands signed the Treaty of Rome, betting its trading future on European integration.

title: Provo protests and a smoke-bombed wedding
description: Anarchist Provos smoke-bombed Princess Beatrix's wedding procession in Amsterdam, announcing the Dutch sixties.

title: Suriname becomes independent
description: Three centuries of Dutch rule in Suriname ended; a third of the population emigrated to the Netherlands around independence.

title: Beatrix crowned amid riots
description: Squatters battled police through Amsterdam on coronation day under the slogan 'no housing, no coronation'.

title: Srebrenica falls under Dutch UN watch
description: Dutch peacekeepers could not prevent the massacre of the enclave they guarded; the trauma later brought down a government.

title: First Swedish railways open
description: The first state trunk lines began the railway network that would knit together Europe's most sparsely settled large country.

title: Nobel patents dynamite
description: Alfred Nobel tamed nitroglycerin into a safe explosive, building the fortune that would endow the prizes bearing his name.

title: Ericsson founded
description: Lars Magnus Ericsson opened a telegraph repair shop in Stockholm that grew into a global telephone giant.

title: Social Democratic party founded
description: The party that would dominate twentieth-century Swedish politics was founded by trade unionists in Stockholm.

title: Nobel signs his will
description: A year before his death, Nobel left nearly his entire fortune to fund annual prizes for those who confer 'the greatest benefit on mankind'.

title: First Nobel Prizes awarded
description: Röntgen, van 't Hoff, and their fellow laureates received the first prizes in Stockholm on the anniversary of Nobel's death.

title: Norway leaves the union peacefully
description: After a Norwegian plebiscite, Sweden let the union dissolve without a shot — a rare peaceful separation in European history.

title: Neutral in the Great War
description: Sweden declared neutrality and held it through four years of war at its doorstep, though the blockade brought hunger by 1917.

title: Universal suffrage
description: Women voted in a national election for the first time, completing Sweden's transition to full parliamentary democracy.

title: Social Democrats begin four decades in power
description: Per Albin Hansson's 'people's home' vision began forty-four nearly unbroken years of Social Democratic government and the modern welfare state.

title: Neutrality under pressure
description: With Denmark and Norway occupied, Sweden balanced concessions to Germany — including troop transit — against staying out of the war.

title: Haven for Denmark's Jews
description: When word of the planned roundup leaked, Danish fishermen ferried more than 7,000 Jews across the Sound to open Swedish harbors.

title: National health insurance for all
description: Compulsory health insurance came into force, a pillar of the welfare state the world came to call the Swedish model.

title: Dagen H: Sweden switches sides
description: At 5 a.m. on 3 September the entire country changed from left- to right-hand traffic — the largest logistical event in its peacetime history.

title: ABBA win Eurovision
description: 'Waterloo' won at Brighton and launched the most successful pop group ever to sing in a second language.

title: Olof Palme assassinated
description: The prime minister was shot dead walking home from the cinema without bodyguards; the murder remains formally unsolved.

title: Sweden joins the European Union
description: After a close referendum, Sweden entered the EU — while keeping its crown and, later, declining the euro.

title: Mickiewicz dies in Constantinople
description: The national poet died of cholera while organizing Polish legions to fight Russia in the Crimean War.

title: January Uprising
description: A guerrilla war against Russian rule burned for over a year; its defeat brought executions, Siberian exile, and harsh Russification.

title: Maria Skłodowska born in Warsaw
description: The future Marie Curie, twice a Nobel laureate, was born under Russian rule in a Warsaw that barred women from its university.

title: Academy of Learning founded in Kraków
description: Under Austria's lighter hand, Kraków's academy became the intellectual capital of a nation without a state.

title: Oxygen liquefied in Kraków
description: Wróblewski and Olszewski were the first to liquefy oxygen from the air in stable form, a landmark of low-temperature physics.

title: Sienkiewicz publishes Quo Vadis
description: The novel of Nero's Rome became a world bestseller and helped win its author the Nobel Prize — Polish literature conquering without an army.

title: Revolution in Congress Poland
description: Strikes and street fighting swept Russian Poland; Piłsudski's fighters and Dmowski's nationalists drew rival maps of the future.

title: Independence regained
description: As the partitioning empires collapsed, Piłsudski took command in Warsaw on 11 November; Poland returned to the map after 123 years.

title: The Miracle on the Vistula
description: At the gates of Warsaw the Polish army broke the Red Army's advance, halting the westward march of the Bolshevik revolution.

title: Germany and the USSR invade
description: The war began at Westerplatte on 1 September; seventeen days later the Soviets took the east, and Poland was partitioned a fourth time.

title: Katyn massacre
description: The Soviet NKVD murdered some 22,000 captured Polish officers and intelligentsia in the Katyn forest and elsewhere, then blamed Germany for fifty years.

title: Warsaw Ghetto Uprising
description: The Jews of the Warsaw ghetto rose against deportation to the death camps and held out for nearly a month against the SS.

title: Warsaw Uprising
description: The Home Army fought for sixty-three days while the Red Army waited across the river; the Germans then razed the city block by block.

title: The country moves west
description: Yalta and Potsdam shifted Poland bodily westward to the Oder-Neisse line, with millions expelled and resettled on both sides.

title: Poznań rising and the thaw
description: Workers' protests in Poznań were shot down in June, but by October Gomułka rode the unrest to power and a brief liberalization.

title: Millennium of Polish Christianity
description: Church and party staged dueling celebrations of Poland's thousandth year, a contest the church quietly won.

title: Massacre on the Baltic coast
description: Troops fired on shipyard workers protesting price rises in Gdańsk and Gdynia, killing dozens and toppling Gomułka.

title: Karol Wojtyła becomes John Paul II
description: The Archbishop of Kraków became the first Slavic pope; his 1979 pilgrimage home told Poles 'be not afraid'.

title: Solidarity founded at the Gdańsk shipyard
description: Lech Wałęsa's strike committee became the Soviet bloc's first independent trade union, ten million strong within a year.

title: Martial law declared
description: General Jaruzelski put tanks on the streets and interned Solidarity's leaders, freezing but not killing the movement.

title: Round Table and the June elections
description: Solidarity swept every contested seat it could stand for; by September Poland had the bloc's first non-communist premier since the 1940s.

title: Poland joins NATO
description: Fifty years after the Iron Curtain fell across it, Poland entered the Atlantic alliance alongside Hungary and the Czech Republic.

title: The Rose-Colored Map
description: Portugal published its claim to a band of Africa stretching from Angola to Mozambique — the ambition Britain would crush with the 1890 Ultimatum.

title: Kangxi confronts the Rites Controversy
description: Kangxi required missionaries to accept Confucian rites as civil, not religious; Rome's refusal soured a century of Jesuit access to the court.

title: Kangxi freezes the poll tax
description: Declaring the empire prosperous, Kangxi fixed head-tax quotas forever — a landmark of Qing statecraft and a future fiscal straitjacket.

title: Yongzheng bans Christian missions
description: The new emperor proscribed Christianity outside the court, closing the door the Rites Controversy had left ajar.

title: Qianlong's long reign begins
description: Qianlong would rule for six decades, presiding over the Qing empire's greatest extent and the start of its long decline.

title: First Jinchuan campaign
description: Qianlong's armies fought hill fortresses in western Sichuan — the first of the costly 'Ten Great Campaigns' he would later celebrate.

title: Tibet under Qing protectorate
description: After intervening in Lhasa's civil strife, the Qing fixed the Dalai Lama's government under imperial residents — the ambans.

title: Xinjiang conquered
description: The destruction of the Dzungars and conquest of the Tarim Basin brought the Qing empire to its greatest extent.

title: Burma campaigns begin
description: Four Qing invasions of Burma ended in costly failure — the limits of the empire's reach, hidden behind official victory rhetoric.

title: Wang Lun's White Lotus rising
description: A martial-arts sect briefly seized cities in Shandong — an early tremor of the sectarian rebellions that would shake the dynasty.

title: The Four Treasuries completed
description: Qianlong's scholars finished copying the vast Siku Quanshu library — while quietly destroying thousands of works deemed seditious.

title: Macartney embassy rebuffed
description: Britain's first embassy was told the Celestial Empire had no need of its manufactures — a famous misreading on both sides.

title: White Lotus Rebellion
description: A decade-long sectarian rebellion drained the treasury Qianlong left behind and exposed the rot in the Qing military.

title: Rebels breach the Forbidden City
description: Eight Trigrams sectarians fought their way into the palace itself before being cut down — an omen the court could not ignore.

title: Grand Canal crisis
description: Yellow River flooding crippled the Grand Canal, forcing grain shipments to sea routes and exposing the empire's decaying waterworks.

title: First Opium War begins
description: Commissioner Lin destroyed British opium at Canton; the gunboats that answered opened China's century of humiliation.

title: Treaty of Nanjing
description: The first 'unequal treaty' ceded Hong Kong, opened five ports, and set the pattern for a century of foreign concessions.

title: The 47 rōnin avenge their lord
description: The masterless samurai of Akō killed the official who had disgraced their lord, then surrendered for sentence — Japan's most retold tale of loyalty.

title: Yoshimune begins the Kyōhō reforms
description: The eighth shogun attacked the regime's debts with austerity, new rice policy, and a petition box for commoners.

title: Ban on Western books relaxed
description: Yoshimune allowed non-Christian European books into Japan, seeding the 'Dutch learning' that would flower for a century.

title: Kyōhō famine
description: Locusts and bad weather brought famine to western Japan, and rice riots to Edo the following year — the first of the era's great subsistence crises.

title: The Kujikata Osadamegaki legal code
description: Yoshimune's jurists completed the shogunate's great judicial code, standardizing crime and punishment across Tokugawa lands.

title: Hōreki incident
description: Kyoto courtiers who lectured the young emperor on imperial restoration were purged by the shogunate — loyalist ideas a century early.

title: Full-color woodblock prints invented
description: Suzuki Harunobu's nishiki-e 'brocade pictures' launched the golden age of ukiyo-e printmaking.

title: Sugita Genpaku's anatomy translation
description: The Kaitai Shinsho, translated from a Dutch anatomy text, proved European science worked — and made Dutch learning respectable.

title: Mount Asama erupts; the Tenmei famine
description: Asama's eruption deepened a famine that killed hundreds of thousands and set off riots that broke the Tanuma government.

title: Russia knocks at the closed door
description: Adam Laxman's embassy to Hokkaido sought trade for Russia; the shogunate's polite refusal opened a half-century of northern anxiety.

title: Rezanov rebuffed at Nagasaki
description: After six months' wait, the Russian envoy was refused trade outright; Russian raiders answered in the north, hardening seclusion.

title: The Golovnin incident
description: Japan imprisoned a Russian captain surveying the Kurils for two years; the affair ended in an uneasy northern truce.

title: Edict to repel foreign ships
description: The shogunate ordered any approaching Western vessel driven off without inquiry — seclusion at its most brittle.

title: Tenpō famine begins
description: Four years of crop failure brought starvation and, in 1837, a former policeman's armed rising in Osaka against official indifference.

title: Tenpō reforms
description: The shogunate's last great reform program — sumptuary laws, forced land transfers, price controls — failed within two years.

title: Death of Aurangzeb
description: The last great Mughal died campaigning in the Deccan; the empire he had overstretched began to fragment within a generation.

title: Company wins Mughal trade privileges
description: Emperor Farrukhsiyar's farman gave the East India Company duty-free trade in Bengal — a paper concession with imperial consequences.

title: The Nizam founds Hyderabad state
description: The Mughal viceroy of the Deccan made himself effectively independent — the pattern by which the empire dissolved into successor states.

title: Nader Shah sacks Delhi
description: The Persian conqueror massacred Delhi and carried off the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor; Mughal prestige never recovered.

title: Maratha raids reach Bengal
description: Maratha horsemen plundered Bengal's countryside for a decade, extracting tribute the enfeebled empire could not prevent.

title: Battle of Plassey
description: Clive's small army and a bought commander won Bengal in an afternoon, opening the East India Company's century of conquest.

title: Third Battle of Panipat
description: The Afghan king shattered Maratha power on the plain of Panipat — clearing, in the end, not his own path but the Company's.

title: The Company wins Bengal's revenues
description: At Allahabad, Shah Alam II granted the Company the diwani of Bengal — a trading firm became the tax collector of twenty million people.

title: Great Bengal famine
description: Perhaps ten million died in Bengal while Company revenue collection continued — a catastrophe that forced Parliament's first oversight of India.

title: Pitt's India Act
description: London placed the Company under a government Board of Control — empire by corporation, now on a parliamentary leash.

title: Tipu Sultan falls at Seringapatam
description: The Tiger of Mysore died defending his capital against the Company; southern India's last great resistance ended with him.

title: The British take Delhi
description: Company armies entered the Mughal capital; the emperor became a Company pensioner ruling from the Red Fort in name alone.

title: Maratha power broken
description: Victory in the Third Anglo-Maratha War left the Company paramount across the subcontinent.

title: Sati abolished
description: Governor-General Bentinck banned widow-burning in Bengal, with the reformer Ram Mohan Roy's campaign behind the law.

title: English made the language of instruction
description: Macaulay's minute set Company education policy on English — shaping the subcontinent's elite, courts, and grievances for generations.

title: Punjab annexed
description: The Sikh Empire fell after the Second Anglo-Sikh War, completing the Company's conquest of the subcontinent's core.

title: Afghan revolt at Kandahar
description: Mirwais Hotak's rising against Safavid misrule created the Afghan power that would destroy the dynasty within a generation.

title: Herat breaks away
description: The Abdali Afghans of Herat threw off Safavid rule — the empire's eastern marches dissolving while Isfahan's court looked inward.

title: The fall of Isfahan
description: An Afghan army starved the magnificent Safavid capital into surrender after a six-month siege; the dynasty never truly recovered.

title: Nader crowned shah
description: The brilliant general who had expelled the Afghans deposed the last Safavid puppet and took the crown on the Moghan plain.

title: The sack of Delhi
description: Nader's Indian campaign brought back the Peacock Throne and treasure enough to remit Persia's taxes for three years.

title: Nader assassinated; the empire dissolves
description: His own officers killed the increasingly cruel conqueror in his tent; his empire fragmented among warlords within months.

title: Karim Khan rules from Shiraz
description: The Zand chief took power calling himself only Vakil — 'representative of the people' — and gave southern Persia a rare generation of peace.

title: Shiraz flourishes under the Vakil
description: Karim Khan rebuilt Shiraz with citadel, bazaar, and mosque — the last flowering of old Persia before the Qajar century.

title: Karim Khan dies; succession wars
description: The Vakil's death loosed a decade of fratricidal war between Zand princes as the Qajar chief watched from the north.

title: Agha Mohammad unifies Persia
description: The ruthless Qajar chief broke the last Zand resistance and reunified Persia from his new capital at Tehran.

title: Qajar coronation
description: Agha Mohammad Khan was crowned shah, founding the dynasty that would rule Persia into the twentieth century.

title: War with Russia begins
description: Persia fought a decade-long war to hold the Caucasus against Russia's advance — and lost.

title: Treaty of Gulistan
description: Persia ceded Georgia, Dagestan, and most of Azerbaijan to Russia — the first great amputation of Qajar territory.

title: Treaty of Turkmenchay
description: A second lost war fixed the Aras River border and gave Russia sweeping privileges inside Persia — a byword for national humiliation ever since.

title: Siege of Herat
description: Mohammad Shah besieged Herat to recover the east; British pressure from the Gulf forced him home — the Great Game in miniature.

title: Amir Kabir begins reforms
description: The great chief minister founded Persia's first modern college and attacked corruption — until court intrigue had him murdered in a bathhouse.

title: White Lotus Rebellion crushed
description: Nine years of campaigning finally broke the rebellion, at a cost of 120 million taels — five years of imperial revenue — and the Qing military's reputation.

title: Taiping Rebellion begins
description: Hong Xiuquan's Heavenly Kingdom rose in the south; the civil war that followed killed more people than any other of the century, anywhere.

title: The Summer Palace burned
description: British and French troops looted and burned the Yuanmingyuan at the close of the Second Opium War — a wound still cited today.

title: Cixi's regency begins
description: The Xianfeng emperor's death left a five-year-old heir; the Empress Dowager Cixi would dominate the court for nearly half a century.

title: China's first railway — promptly bought and torn up
description: Foreign merchants built the Woosung line without permission; Qing officials purchased it and demolished it the next year.

title: Sino-French War
description: France destroyed the new Fuzhou fleet in an hour and took Vietnam from the Qing tributary system.

title: Defeat by Japan
description: The First Sino-Japanese War ended with Taiwan ceded and the fiction of Qing power destroyed — beaten by the neighbor it had long condescended to.

title: Boxer Rebellion
description: Anti-foreign Boxers besieged the legations with court blessing; eight foreign armies took Beijing and imposed a crushing indemnity.

title: Xinhai Revolution
description: A mutiny at Wuchang toppled two millennia of imperial rule in four months; the boy emperor Puyi abdicated the following February.

title: May Fourth Movement
description: Students marched against the Versailles betrayal of Shandong; the movement's ferment produced both modern Chinese culture and the Communist Party.

title: Shanghai massacre
description: Chiang Kai-shek turned on his Communist allies mid-Northern Expedition, opening two decades of civil war.

title: The Long March
description: The encircled Red Army broke out and marched 9,000 kilometers to Yan'an; Mao emerged from the ordeal as the party's leader.

title: Japan invades; the Nanjing Massacre
description: Full-scale war began at the Marco Polo Bridge; that winter Japanese troops murdered and raped through the captured capital for weeks.

title: The People's Republic proclaimed
description: Mao declared the People's Republic from Tiananmen; Chiang's government withdrew to Taiwan.

title: Great Leap Forward
description: Mao's crash collectivization and backyard steel campaign produced the deadliest famine in recorded history.

title: Cultural Revolution launched
description: Mao unleashed the Red Guards against the party itself; a decade of persecution and chaos followed.

title: Nixon in China
description: The American president's week in Beijing reversed a quarter-century of hostility and reshaped the Cold War.

title: Deng opens the economy
description: The Third Plenum embraced 'reform and opening up'; special economic zones and de-collectivized farms began the fastest enrichment in history.

title: Tiananmen Square
description: The army cleared weeks of student-led protests from central Beijing with tanks and live fire on the night of June 3–4.

title: Hong Kong returns
description: Britain's lease expired and the colony became a Special Administrative Region under 'one country, two systems'.

title: Perry's black ships
description: Four American warships in Edo Bay demanded the country open; the shogunate's inability to refuse began its unraveling.

title: Meiji Restoration
description: Young samurai overthrew the shogunate in the emperor's name and set out to remake Japan on Western industrial lines.

title: Satsuma Rebellion
description: Saigō Takamori's samurai rising — the last stand of the old warrior class — was crushed by the new conscript army.

title: Meiji Constitution
description: Japan became Asia's first constitutional state, with a parliament — and sovereignty vested firmly in the emperor.

title: Victory over China
description: Japan took Taiwan and shocked the world by beating the Qing — then was forced by three European powers to hand back its mainland prize.

title: Victory over Russia
description: Tsushima and Mukden made Japan the first Asian power to defeat a European empire in modern war.

title: Korea annexed
description: Japan formally absorbed Korea, beginning thirty-five years of colonial rule that still shadows the region.

title: Great Kantō earthquake
description: Quake and firestorm killed over 100,000 in Tokyo and Yokohama; mobs murdered thousands of Koreans amid the panic.

title: Manchuria seized
description: The Kwantung Army staged a bombing and conquered Manchuria without Tokyo's sanction — the military now steering the state.

title: Pearl Harbor
description: The carrier strike on the U.S. Pacific Fleet opened a war across half the globe that Japan's planners knew it could not win long.

title: Hiroshima, Nagasaki, surrender
description: Two atomic bombs and the Soviet declaration ended the war; the emperor's radio address asked his people to 'endure the unendurable'.

title: The postwar constitution
description: The occupation-drafted constitution renounced war forever and made the emperor a symbol — the frame of Japanese democracy since.

title: The miracle economy takes off
description: With the '1955 system' of LDP rule in place, Japan entered two decades of near-ten-percent growth that remade world industry.

title: Tokyo Olympics and the Shinkansen
description: The Games announced Japan's return to the world; the bullet train that opened days before became its emblem.

title: Okinawa returned
description: The United States handed Okinawa back after twenty-seven years — with its bases, and its grievances, remaining.

title: The Shōwa era ends
description: Hirohito died after sixty-two years on the throne — the twentieth century's longest reign, spanning militarism, ruin, and the miracle.

title: Kobe earthquake and the subway attack
description: Six thousand died in the Kobe quake; weeks later the Aum cult's sarin attack on the Tokyo subway shattered the myth of safe Japan.

title: First railway, Bombay to Thane
description: India's first passenger train ran 34 kilometers; within decades the subcontinent had the largest rail network in Asia.

title: The Rebellion of 1857
description: Sepoy mutiny became a general rising across northern India; its suppression was savage, and the last Mughal was exiled to Rangoon.

title: Crown rule replaces the Company
description: Parliament dissolved the East India Company's rule; India passed to the British Crown — the Raj proper begins.

title: Gandhi born at Porbandar
description: Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi was born on the Kathiawar coast; his weapon of mass nonviolence would undo an empire.

title: Victoria proclaimed Empress of India
description: The Royal Titles Act crowned the Raj's pageantry while famine killed millions in the Deccan the same year.

title: Indian National Congress founded
description: Seventy-two delegates met in Bombay to petition for a voice in government; the body they founded would one day inherit the Raj.

title: Plague reaches Bombay
description: The third plague pandemic killed millions across India over the following decades; heavy-handed containment bred political anger.

title: Bengal partitioned
description: Curzon split Bengal along communal lines; the Swadeshi boycott that answered was nationalism's first mass campaign.

title: Amritsar massacre
description: Dyer's troops fired into a trapped crowd at Jallianwala Bagh, killing hundreds — the moment, many said later, the Raj lost India.

title: The Salt March
description: Gandhi walked 240 miles to make salt from the sea in defiance of the salt tax, and the world watched the Raj arrest 60,000 people.

title: Bengal famine
description: Some three million died in wartime Bengal while grain exports and denial policies continued — a scar on the Raj's final years.

title: Independence and Partition
description: At midnight on 15 August India was free — and divided; Partition's migrations and massacres killed perhaps a million people.

title: Gandhi assassinated
description: A Hindu nationalist shot Gandhi at his prayer meeting in Delhi, five and a half months into the freedom he had won.

title: The Republic's constitution
description: Ambedkar's constitution — the world's longest — made India a sovereign democratic republic with universal suffrage from day one.

title: War with China
description: China's month-long offensive in the Himalayas humiliated Nehru's India and hardened its defense posture for decades.

title: Green Revolution begins
description: High-yield wheat and new irrigation turned chronic famine into grain surplus within a decade, transforming Punjab first.

title: Bangladesh war
description: India's two-week victory over Pakistan midwifed independent Bangladesh and made Indira Gandhi briefly untouchable.

title: The Emergency
description: Indira Gandhi suspended civil liberties for twenty-one months — democracy's closest brush with extinction in India, ended by her own election call.

title: Indira Gandhi assassinated
description: Her Sikh bodyguards killed her months after the army stormed the Golden Temple; anti-Sikh pogroms followed in Delhi.

title: Economic liberalization
description: A balance-of-payments crisis forced open the License Raj; Manmohan Singh's reforms began India's high-growth era.

title: Pokhran nuclear tests
description: Five underground tests declared India a nuclear power; Pakistan answered within weeks.

title: Anglo-Persian War over Herat
description: Britain landed in the Gulf to force Persia out of Herat; the peace made Persia renounce Afghanistan forever.

title: The telegraph crosses Persia
description: The Indo-European telegraph line put Tehran on the wire between London and Calcutta — modernity arriving as an imperial through-route.

title: The Reuter concession
description: Naser al-Din sold a British baron rights to nearly everything — railways, mines, the national bank; outrage at home and in Russia killed it.

title: Imperial Bank of Persia founded
description: A British-chartered bank won the note-issuing monopoly — foreign concessions tightening even as the tobacco storm gathered.

title: The tobacco protest
description: A clerical fatwa against the British tobacco monopoly emptied every pipe in Persia — even the shah's harem — until the concession fell.

title: Constitutional Revolution
description: Mass protest won Persia a constitution and parliament — the Majles — from a dying shah; the struggle to keep them defined the next decades.

title: Oil struck at Masjed Soleyman
description: The Middle East's first oil strike created Anglo-Persian — later BP — and made Persia's politics a matter of imperial strategy.

title: The Shuster affair
description: A Russian ultimatum forced out the American reformer hired to fix Persia's finances; Russian troops shelled the Majles's defenders.

title: Reza Khan's coup
description: A Cossack colonel marched on Tehran and took the war ministry; within four years he would take the crown.

title: Reza Shah crowned
description: The Majles deposed the absent Qajar and crowned Reza Shah Pahlavi, who set about building a centralized, secular state at speed.

title: 'Iran' for the world
description: Tehran asked foreign governments to use the country's own name — Iran — in place of Persia.

title: Anglo-Soviet invasion
description: Britain and the USSR occupied neutral Iran to secure the supply corridor to Russia; Reza Shah was forced out in favor of his son.

title: Oil nationalized
description: Mossadegh's parliament nationalized Anglo-Iranian Oil, electrifying the decolonizing world and enraging London.

title: Mossadegh overthrown
description: A CIA- and MI6-organized coup restored the shah's power; the memory would poison U.S.–Iranian relations permanently.

title: White Revolution
description: The shah's land reform and modernization drive uprooted old elites — and made an exile of a cleric named Khomeini.

title: Revolution gathers
description: A year of swelling protest, massacre, and strikes broke the shah's rule; he left Iran in January 1979, never to return.

title: Islamic Revolution; the hostage crisis
description: Khomeini returned to found the Islamic Republic; that November students seized the U.S. embassy and held 52 hostages for 444 days.

title: Iran-Iraq War begins
description: Saddam's invasion opened eight years of trench war, missile strikes on cities, and perhaps a million dead.

title: The war ends
description: Khomeini accepted the UN ceasefire — 'more deadly than drinking poison', he said — with the border back where it started.

title: Khatami's reform landslide
description: A reformist cleric won the presidency with seventy percent of the vote, briefly opening Iran's press and politics.

title: Non-cooperation movement launched
description: Gandhi turned Congress from petitions to mass refusal — boycotts of courts, schools, and cloth that made nationalism a village-level force.

title: Iyasu I assassinated
description: The great reforming emperor was murdered in a plot around his own son — the opening act of Gondar's century of palace intrigue.

title: Dawit III crowned amid intrigue
description: Another Gondarine emperor raised by factions and dead within five years — poisoned, the chronicles say.

title: Bakaffa's reign of secrets
description: Bakaffa ruled through informers and sudden purges, and wandered his realm in disguise — the stuff of Ethiopia's most told royal tales.

title: Mentewab's regency begins
description: The empress mother Mentewab dominated her son Iyasu II's reign, building Gondar's finest churches while the provinces slipped away.

title: Disaster on the Sennar campaign
description: Iyasu II's invasion of Sennar ended in rout on the Dinder; the emperor barely escaped and imperial prestige sank further.

title: Iyasu II dies; Oromo power rises
description: The succession passed through Oromo royal marriages, shifting court power toward new kingmakers.

title: The Zemene Mesafint begins
description: Ras Mikael Sehul murdered one emperor and installed another; for nearly a century the emperors of Gondar would be warlords' puppets.

title: James Bruce reaches Gondar
description: The Scottish traveler reached the springs of the Blue Nile and brought back tales of Gondar that Europe refused to believe.

title: The Yejju lords take the regency
description: Ali I of Yejju made himself Ras of the empire; his Oromo dynasty would rule through puppet emperors for two generations.

title: Emperors made and unmade
description: Tekle Giyorgis I was crowned and deposed repeatedly — six reigns in all — as the warlords traded the throne among themselves.

title: Ras Gugsa's long dominance
description: From Debre Tabor, Gugsa of Yejju ruled the empire in all but name for a quarter century of the Era of Princes.

title: Birth of Kassa Hailu
description: In Qwara on the western marches was born the shifta's son who would end the Era of Princes as Emperor Tewodros II.

title: Egypt conquers the Sudan next door
description: Muhammad Ali's armies took Sennar and the Nile borderlands, bringing an expansionist Egypt to Ethiopia's western frontier.

title: Battle of Debre Abbay
description: The lords of Tigray and the Yejju regents bled each other in one of the Era of Princes' greatest battles.

title: Kassa turns bandit chief
description: Denied his inheritance, Kassa Hailu built a shifta army in the lowlands — the following he would ride to the throne.

title: The Arma pay tribute in all directions
description: The descendants of Morocco's conquerors still held Timbuktu's citadel, but bought peace from Tuareg, Bambara, and Fulani alike.

title: Biton Kulibali founds the Segu empire
description: A Bambara hunters' association became a kingdom; Segu's war canoes would dominate the middle Niger for a century.

title: Bambara power spreads along the river
description: Segu's armies raided from Jenne toward Timbuktu, squeezing the last authority from the Arma pashas.

title: Tuareg victory at Toya
description: The Tuareg crushed the pashalik's army near Timbuktu; the desert lords now dictated terms on the Niger bend.

title: Famine on the Niger bend
description: The chronicles of Timbuktu record years of famine and plague at mid-century, emptying towns along the great river.

title: Death of Biton Kulibali
description: Segu's founder died and his state convulsed; the tonjon war-slaves would soon crown kings of their own.

title: Tuareg take Gao
description: The old Songhai capital fell under direct Tuareg control as the pashalik's remnant authority withered to Timbuktu's walls.

title: The pashalik's tribute ends
description: Tuareg lords ended the fiction of Moroccan-descended rule at Timbuktu; the Arma became one faction among many.

title: Mungo Park reaches the Niger
description: The Scottish surgeon confirmed the great river flowed east — the first of the European probes that would end in conquest.

title: Mungo Park dies at Bussa
description: Park's second expedition ended with his death in the Bussa rapids after running a gauntlet down a thousand miles of river.

title: Seku Amadu's jihad founds Massina
description: Victory at Noukouma launched a Fulani theocracy over the inland delta, with a new capital rising at Hamdullahi.

title: Laing reaches Timbuktu
description: The first European known to reach the fabled city was murdered leaving it; the mystery of Timbuktu only deepened.

title: Caillié returns alive
description: Disguised as a Muslim traveler, René Caillié reached Timbuktu and lived to publish the truth: the golden city was mudbrick and sand.

title: Hamdullahi in its prime
description: Massina's purpose-built capital ran on grain tithes, Qur'anic courts, and a welfare register — a planned Islamic state in the delta.

title: Umar Tall preaches from Futa Jallon
description: Back from Mecca with a Tijani mandate, al-Hajj Umar gathered disciples and guns — the next jihad was forming.

title: Our Lady of Guadalupe
description: The apparition to Juan Diego on Tepeyac hill gave New Spain its patroness and Mexican identity its most enduring symbol.

title: Silver found at Zacatecas
description: The strike at Zacatecas opened the silver veins that would bankroll the Spanish empire for two centuries.

title: University founded in Mexico City
description: The Royal University of Mexico opened its doors — with Lima's, the first university of the Americas.

title: The Manila galleon route opens
description: Urdaneta found the way back across the Pacific; for 250 years Acapulco's galleons tied New Spain to Asia.

title: The Inquisition established
description: The Holy Office opened its Mexico City tribunal, policing the faith of the colony — though not of its indigenous converts.

title: Third Mexican Council
description: The colonial church codified its rules for a century to come, debating — and dodging — the treatment of Indian labor.

title: The Alameda laid out
description: The viceroy ordered a poplar promenade at the city's edge — the oldest public park in the Americas.

title: The great drainage begun
description: Tens of thousands of Indian laborers dug the Huehuetoca canal to drain the Valley of Mexico — a war on water that lasted centuries.

title: Tepehuán revolt
description: A messianic rising in the northern sierra killed missionaries and settlers before its brutal suppression.

title: Riot deposes a viceroy
description: A Mexico City crowd, backed by the archbishop's faction, stormed the palace and drove the Marquis of Gelves from office.

title: The great flood
description: Mexico City drowned under five years of standing water; abandoning the capital was seriously debated.

title: The waters recede
description: After five years the flood withdrew and the drainage works were redoubled — the lake city permanently remaking its ground.

title: Palafox against the Jesuits
description: Bishop Palafox of Puebla fought viceroys and the Society of Jesus alike — colonial church politics at their fiercest.

title: The cathedral consecrated
description: After a century of building, Mexico City's cathedral was consecrated — the largest church in the Americas, still unfinished.

title: Sigüenza y Góngora's chair
description: The creole polymath took the chair of mathematics and astrology — colonial science between comets, maps, and Aztec antiquities.

title: Pirates sack Veracruz
description: Laurens de Graaf's buccaneers held the port city for days, locking its people in the cathedral while they stripped it bare.

title: The corn riot
description: Amid famine, a crowd burned the viceroy's palace and the city hall; Sigüenza rescued the archives from the flames.

title: Death of Sor Juana
description: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz, the greatest poet of the colonial Americas, died nursing plague victims, her library already sold off.

title: New Spain proclaims the Bourbon king
description: The colony swore loyalty to Philip V; the dynastic change in Madrid would eventually remake colonial government itself.

title: Earthquake shakes the capital
description: A strong quake damaged the sinking lakebed city — one of the recurring blows of its improbable geography.

title: First newspaper of New Spain
description: The Gaceta de México began publishing — news of fleets, sermons, and silver for the colonial elite.

title: The matlazahuatl epidemic
description: Typhus killed perhaps a third of central Mexico's Indian population; Guadalupe was sworn the city's patroness in supplication.

title: Famine year
description: Drought and frost brought hunger across the Bajío — the boom-bust rhythm of the colonial countryside.

title: Guadalupe made patroness of New Spain
description: Rome confirmed the Virgin of Guadalupe as patroness — the creole church's proudest cause triumphant.

title: Jesuits expelled from New Spain
description: The Society's expulsion sparked riots in the Bajío, hanged rioters — and a generation of exiled creole Jesuit intellectuals.

title: Free trade within the empire
description: The Bourbon decree ended Cádiz's monopoly; Veracruz boomed and the old consulado order began to crack.

title: The year of hunger
description: The worst famine of the colonial era killed perhaps 300,000; the intendancy reforms arrived the same year.

title: The Sun Stone unearthed
description: Pavers in the Zócalo turned up the great Aztec calendar stone; creole scholars began reclaiming the pre-conquest past.

title: Humboldt in New Spain
description: Humboldt's year of surveys produced the famous Political Essay — and told the world how rich, and how unequal, New Spain was.

title: The Grito de Dolores
description: Father Hidalgo rang his parish bell and raised an army of tens of thousands under Guadalupe's banner — independence had begun.

title: Morelos executed
description: The insurgency's greatest general was shot at San Cristóbal Ecatepec; the cause guttered into guerrilla war.

title: Independence — the Plan of Iguala
description: Iturbide's three guarantees united insurgents and royalists; the Army of the Three Guarantees entered Mexico City in September.

title: The federal republic's constitution
description: After Iturbide's brief empire, Mexico constituted itself a federal republic under the charter of 1824.

title: Texas lost
description: San Jacinto ended Santa Anna's Texas campaign in defeat and capture; the secession would lead to a far greater war.

title: The US army takes Mexico City
description: Scott's army fought its way from Veracruz to the capital; the boy cadets of Chapultepec entered national legend.

title: Guadalupe Hidalgo — half the country ceded
description: The treaty surrendered the northern territories from California to New Mexico — over half of Mexico's land, for fifteen million dollars.

title: Toledo's reordering of the Andes
description: Viceroy Toledo left Peru having herded native communities into planned towns and bound them to the Potosí mita — the colonial system set in stone.

title: Alcabala revolt in Quito
description: The viceroyalty's northern city rose against a new sales tax; the crown answered with troops and pardons.

title: Huaynaputina erupts
description: The largest South American eruption in history buried Arequipa's countryside and chilled climates around the globe.

title: Guaman Poma writes to the king
description: The Andean chronicler finished his thousand-page illustrated letter on colonial abuses; it went unread for three centuries.

title: Potosí's dam disaster
description: The Caricari reservoir burst and swept away mills and thousands of lives in the silver city — richest and cruelest town on earth.

title: The great auto-da-fé of Lima
description: The Inquisition burned eleven at Lima's greatest auto — most of them Portuguese merchants condemned as secret Jews.

title: Callao walled against the Dutch
description: After Dutch raids, Lima's port was ringed with fortifications — the Pacific was no longer a Spanish lake.

title: Cuzco earthquake
description: The old Inca capital was shattered; its baroque rebuilding gave Cuzco the face it wears today.

title: Rose of Lima beatified
description: The Lima mystic became the first American-born candidate for sainthood — creole pride found its heavenly champion.

title: First saint of the Americas
description: Rose of Lima was canonized and proclaimed patroness of the New World.

title: Lima earthquake
description: The quake wrecked Lima and Callao and broke the coastal wheat economy; Chile became Peru's granary.

title: University of San Antonio Abad
description: Cuzco gained its own university — Andean letters no longer had to travel to Lima.

title: French traders flood the coast
description: With a Bourbon on the Spanish throne, French ships rounded the Horn and sold openly in Pacific ports — the monopoly leaking at every seam.

title: Utrecht opens the door wider
description: The asiento and the annual ship handed British traders a legal wedge into Spanish American commerce.

title: Plague in Cuzco
description: An epidemic killed tens of thousands in the Cuzco region — one of the colonial Andes' worst demographic blows.

title: New Granada carved away
description: The northern provinces became a separate viceroyalty — the first great reduction of Lima's dominion.

title: Juan Santos Atahualpa rises
description: A messianic rebel claiming Inca descent drove the missions from the central jungle — and was never captured.

title: Lima destroyed; Callao drowned
description: Earthquake leveled Lima and a tsunami erased Callao, killing thousands — the viceroyalty's greatest catastrophe.

title: Lima conspiracy uncovered
description: An indigenous plot to rise against the mita and tribute was betrayed and its leaders executed — a tremor before the great revolt.

title: Jesuits expelled from Peru
description: The Society's haciendas and colleges were seized across the viceroyalty; Andean education never quite recovered.

title: Potosí lost to Buenos Aires
description: Upper Peru and its silver went to the new Río de la Plata viceroyalty — Lima's commercial primacy broken at a stroke.

title: Túpac Amaru II's great revolt
description: A kuraka claiming Inca descent raised the southern Andes against colonial abuse; the rising and its repression killed perhaps 100,000.

title: The Mercurio Peruano
description: Lima's enlightened journal mapped, counted, and described Peru for its own creole readers — a nation imagining itself.

title: The royalist bastion strikes back
description: While juntas rose in Quito and La Paz, viceregal Peru crushed them — Lima would be Spain's last stronghold in South America.

title: The Cuzco rebellion
description: Creoles and the aged kuraka Pumacahua raised the southern highlands for the liberal cause; defeat at Umachiri ended it.

title: San Martín proclaims independence
description: 'Peru is from this moment free and independent' — proclaimed in Lima's plaza, though the highlands remained royalist.

title: Ayacucho seals independence
description: Sucre's victory on the Ayacucho plain ended Spanish power on the continent — fought in the old heartland of the Incas.

title: The Peru-Bolivian Confederation
description: Santa Cruz united Peru and Bolivia under his protectorate; Chile went to war to break it within three years.

title: The guano boom
description: Seabird islands made Peru suddenly rich; the guano age paid for railways, abolition — and spectacular waste.

title: The false Inca of the jungle
description: The Andalusian adventurer Pedro Bohórquez had himself crowned Inca by the Calchaquí — colonial Peru's strangest imposture.

title: The desert closes in on the river trade
description: Tuareg confederations squeezed the Niger commerce from both banks; Timbuktu's merchants paid whoever was strongest that season.

title: The cathedral dedicated
description: Mexico City's cathedral was solemnly dedicated, its interior at last complete after more than a century of work.

title: Tewodros II reunifies the empire
description: The bandit chief Kassa defeated the last great warlords and was crowned Tewodros II, ending the Era of Princes.

title: Magdala: Tewodros's last stand
description: Napier's expedition stormed the mountain fortress to free European hostages; Tewodros shot himself rather than be taken.

title: Egypt invades — and is destroyed
description: Yohannes IV annihilated two Egyptian armies at Gundet and Gura, ending Cairo's dream of a Nile-to-sea empire.

title: Menelik II and the Treaty of Wichale
description: The new emperor signed a friendship treaty whose Italian text secretly claimed a protectorate — the fraud that led to war.

title: Battle of Adwa
description: Menelik's army destroyed the Italian invasion force — the great African victory over a European colonial army, and Ethiopia's guarantee of independence.

title: The powers agree to disagree
description: Britain, France, and Italy signed a tripartite pact carving spheres of influence in Ethiopia — without asking Ethiopia.

title: The railway reaches Addis Ababa
description: The French line from Djibouti finally reached the capital, plugging the highland empire into world trade.

title: Ethiopia joins the League of Nations
description: Regent Tafari won admission to the League — the recognition that would later make Italy's invasion the League's great test, and failure.

title: Haile Selassie crowned
description: Tafari became Haile Selassie I, King of Kings — modernizer, autocrat, and to some across the Atlantic, a living messiah.

title: Italy invades
description: Mussolini's armies attacked with aircraft and poison gas; the emperor's appeal to the League of Nations went unanswered.

title: Liberation
description: British and Ethiopian patriot forces drove out the Italians; Haile Selassie re-entered Addis Ababa five years to the day after its fall.

title: Eritrea federated
description: The UN federated the former Italian colony with Ethiopia; its absorption a decade later ignited Africa's longest war.

title: The OAU founded at Addis
description: Thirty-two states founded the Organisation of African Unity in Haile Selassie's capital — decolonized Africa's diplomatic home.

title: Revolution deposes the emperor
description: Famine, mutiny, and strikes brought down the ancient monarchy; the Derg drove Haile Selassie from his palace in a Volkswagen.

title: Famine
description: War and drought brought a famine that killed hundreds of thousands and seared Ethiopia into the world's conscience.

title: The Derg falls
description: Rebel armies took Addis Ababa and Mengistu fled to Zimbabwe; Eritrea's independence followed by referendum.

title: War with Eritrea
description: A border dispute at Badme became a trench war that killed tens of thousands between two of the world's poorest states.

title: Barth's year in Timbuktu
description: Heinrich Barth spent months in the city and copied its chronicles — Europe's first real look at Songhai's written history.

title: Umar takes Massina
description: The Toucouleur jihad conquered Hamdullahi — Muslim empire against Muslim empire, and the delta in flames.

title: The Toucouleur succession wars
description: Ahmadu Tall fought his brothers for his father's empire while the French pushed up the Senegal behind them.

title: The French reach Bamako
description: Borgnis-Desbordes planted the flag on the Niger; the railway and the gunboats were right behind.

title: The French take Timbuktu
description: Columns under Joffre occupied the fabled city; the Toucouleur empire had already collapsed before them.

title: Samory captured
description: The capture of Samory Touré ended the last great resistance to French conquest in the western Sudan.

title: French West Africa organized
description: The conquered territories were bundled into a federation ruled from Dakar; the Niger bend became an administrative hinterland.

title: The Volta-Bani rising
description: One of colonial Africa's largest revolts swept the region south of the bend against wartime conscription and taxes.

title: French Sudan reconstituted
description: The colony took the name Soudan Français and its lasting borders — the mold of the future Mali.

title: The Office du Niger
description: France began its grand irrigation scheme on the inland delta — canals and cotton where Massina's tithe barns had stood.

title: African deputies in Paris
description: The new French Union gave the colonies representation; the US-RDA party began organizing the road to independence.

title: Independence as Mali
description: After the Mali Federation with Senegal broke up, the Sudanese Republic declared itself Mali — reclaiming a medieval empire's name.

title: Traoré's coup
description: Lieutenant Moussa Traoré overthrew Modibo Keïta's socialist government; he would rule for twenty-three years.

title: The Sahel drought
description: Years of failed rains killed herds and people across the Sahel; famine relief became a permanent fixture of the region.

title: The March Revolution
description: Protests and a reformist coup ended Traoré's dictatorship; a national conference wrote one of Africa's most democratic constitutions, and Konaré won the 1992 vote.

title: The Sudanese Republic
description: French Sudan voted itself autonomous within the French Community — the last stop before independence.

title: The Christmas War with Burkina
description: Mali and Burkina Faso fought five days over the Agacher strip; the World Court later split the difference.

title: The liberal constitution
description: The 1857 charter curbed church and army privilege — and triggered three years of civil war between liberals and conservatives.

title: Cinco de Mayo
description: Zaragoza's army beat the French at Puebla — a victory the intervention soon reversed, and a holiday that never faded.

title: Maximilian executed
description: The republic restored, Juárez had the Habsburg emperor shot on the Hill of Bells — Europe's last Mexican adventure over.

title: Díaz takes power
description: Porfirio Díaz's rebellion began three and a half decades of order, railways, foreign capital — and fixed elections.

title: The railway age
description: The Mexico City–El Paso trunk line and its siblings stitched the country to the US market — the Porfiriato's proudest boast.

title: The first national census
