description: Mexico counted itself: twelve and a half million people, four in five of them rural.

title: The Cananea strike
description: Miners struck the American-owned copper pit at Cananea and were shot down — a spark on the Porfiriato's dry tinder.

title: The Revolution begins
description: Madero's call to arms toppled Díaz within months — and opened a decade of revolution that killed perhaps a million Mexicans.

title: The Querétaro constitution
description: Land reform, labor rights, and state control of subsoil wealth — the Revolution written into the world's most radical constitution to date.

title: The Cristero War
description: Enforcement of the anticlerical laws ignited three years of rural Catholic rebellion in the center-west.

title: Oil nationalized
description: Cárdenas expropriated the foreign oil companies and founded Pemex; crowds paid into the compensation fund with jewelry and chickens.

title: Mexico enters the war
description: U-boat sinkings of Mexican tankers brought a declaration of war; Squadron 201 flew in the Pacific and braceros went north.

title: Women win the vote
description: Mexican women gained national suffrage — the PRI era's most consequential reform to its own electorate.

title: Tlatelolco massacre
description: Days before the Olympics, troops fired on students in the Plaza de las Tres Culturas; the toll is contested to this day.

title: The oil boom — and the peso crash
description: Cantarell's vast offshore field promised to 'administer the abundance' just as the first great devaluation hit.

title: The Mexico City earthquake
description: Ten thousand died as the lakebed amplified the shock; the volunteer brigades that dug survivors out reshaped civil society.

title: NAFTA — and the Zapatistas
description: The trade pact took effect on New Year's Day; the same morning, masked rebels seized towns in Chiapas in indigenous protest.

title: The PRI loses
description: Vicente Fox's victory ended seventy-one years of one-party rule — the longest-governing party of the century voted out peacefully.

title: South America's first railway
description: The Lima–Callao line opened, paid for with guano money — the start of Peru's extravagant railway age.

title: Callao repels the Spanish fleet
description: Peru's shore batteries drove off Spain's Pacific squadron on the Dos de Mayo — the old empire's last shot at its former colonies.

title: The War of the Pacific
description: Chile's war over nitrates engulfed Peru and Bolivia; Lima itself would fall within two years.

title: The Treaty of Ancón
description: Peru ceded Tarapacá and its nitrate wealth to Chile — defeat, occupation, and a generation of national soul-searching.

title: Piérola rebuilds the state
description: The 'Caliph' rode into Lima at the head of his montoneros and began the reconstruction that opened Peru's Aristocratic Republic.

title: The Putumayo rubber scandal
description: The rubber boom's atrocities against Amazonian peoples, exposed by Casement's reports, disgraced the Peruvian Amazon Company.

title: Machu Picchu revealed
description: Hiram Bingham was led to the vine-covered citadel above the Urubamba — the lost city of the Incas found, never having been lost to its neighbors.

title: APRA founded
description: From Mexican exile, Haya de la Torre founded the continental party that would dominate — and be banned from — Peruvian politics for decades.

title: The Trujillo uprising
description: APRA's rising in Trujillo and its suppression left a thousand dead and a blood feud between the party and the army.

title: War with Ecuador
description: A short border war ended with the Rio Protocol fixing — for a while — the disputed Amazonian frontier.

title: Odría's coup
description: General Odría seized power against APRA; his ochenio mixed repression with public works and boom-era spending.

title: Civilian rule restored
description: Odría kept his promise to hold elections and stepped down — a rare voluntary exit in the era of caudillos.

title: Velasco's revolution from above
description: The generals seized IPC's oilfields and decreed the hemisphere's most sweeping land reform — a military government of the left.

title: The Ancash earthquake
description: The quake and the Huascarán avalanche it loosed killed some 70,000 — the deadliest disaster in the hemisphere's history.

title: Sendero Luminoso begins
description: The Shining Path burned ballot boxes in Chuschi on election eve, opening an internal war that would kill 70,000 people.

title: The self-coup and Guzmán's capture
description: Fujimori shut congress with the army's backing in April; in September, police lifted Sendero's leader from a Lima safe house.

title: Fujimori falls
description: The bribery videos of his spy chief brought down the regime; Fujimori faxed his resignation from Japan.

title: America gets its name
description: The Waldseemüller map labeled the new continent 'America' after Amerigo Vespucci — and the name stuck.

title: Ponce de León claims Florida
description: The Spanish explorer waded ashore near present-day St. Augustine and claimed La Florida for Spain.

title: Verrazzano scouts the coast
description: Sailing for France, Verrazzano ran the Atlantic coast from the Carolinas to Maine and into New York harbor.

title: De Soto lands in Florida
description: De Soto's entrada wandered the Southeast for four years, spreading devastation and disease far ahead of any colony.

title: Coronado seeks the golden cities
description: Coronado's expedition crossed the Southwest as far as Kansas and found pueblos instead of gold.

title: Pensacola attempted
description: Tristán de Luna's colony at Pensacola Bay failed within two years — the Gulf coast resisted settlement.

title: St. Augustine founded
description: Menéndez planted the oldest continuously inhabited European city in the future United States — and destroyed the French at Fort Caroline.

title: Drake claims New Albion
description: Drake careened the Golden Hind on the California coast and claimed it for Elizabeth — a gesture two centuries early.

title: Roanoke planted
description: Raleigh's colonists settled Roanoke Island; within five years every one of them had vanished.

title: The Lost Colony
description: John White returned to find Roanoke abandoned, the single word CROATOAN carved on a post.

title: Jamestown
description: The Virginia Company's foothold on the James River survived famine and siege to become the first lasting English colony.

title: Hudson sails his river
description: Seeking the Northwest Passage for the Dutch, Hudson instead opened the river — and the fur trade that built New Netherland.

title: Assembly and slavery arrive together
description: Virginia's first representative assembly met at Jamestown the same summer the first captive Africans were sold there.

title: The Mayflower Compact
description: The Pilgrims signed their covenant of self-government in Provincetown harbor before landing at Plymouth.

title: Manhattan purchased
description: Minuit bought Manhattan from the Lenape for sixty guilders of trade goods — the founding bargain of New Amsterdam.

title: A city upon a hill
description: Winthrop's fleet carried a thousand Puritans to Massachusetts Bay, the vanguard of the Great Migration.

title: Harvard founded
description: Massachusetts voted funds for a college at New Towne six years after landing — books before roads.

title: New England confederates
description: Four Puritan colonies formed a defensive league — the first American experiment in union.

title: First Jewish community
description: Twenty-three refugees from Dutch Brazil landed at New Amsterdam and stayed over Stuyvesant's objection.

title: New Amsterdam becomes New York
description: An English fleet took the Dutch colony without a shot; the duke of York gave it his name.

title: King Philip's War
description: Metacom's war burned half the towns of New England — per capita the bloodiest war in American history.

title: Bacon's Rebellion
description: Frontier planters burned Jamestown itself; the rising's aftermath hardened Virginia's turn to African slavery.

title: Philadelphia founded
description: Penn laid out his green country town as a holy experiment in toleration.

title: Salem witch trials
description: Twenty people were executed on spectral evidence before the panic collapsed — a founding cautionary tale.

title: Cabral's landfall
description: Blown west en route to India, Cabral's fleet raised the coast of Brazil and claimed it for Portugal.

title: First sugar mill
description: Sugar planted on the coast began the engine of colonial Brazil — and of the slave trade that powered it.

title: The brazilwood coast
description: French and Portuguese traders bartered for the red dyewood that gave the land its name.

title: São Vicente founded
description: Martim Afonso planted Portugal's first formal settlement on the southern coast.

title: The captaincies
description: John III carved the coast into hereditary captaincies — colonization on the cheap, and mostly a failure.

title: Salvador, first capital
description: Tomé de Sousa founded Salvador as royal capital; the first Jesuits landed with him.

title: São Paulo founded
description: Jesuits founded a mission school on the Piratininga plateau; a metropolis would grow from it.

title: France Antarctique
description: French colonists seized Guanabara Bay; it took Portugal a decade to expel them and found Rio on the spot.

title: Rio de Janeiro founded
description: Estácio de Sá founded Rio to anchor Guanabara Bay against the French.

title: Sugar overtakes dyewood
description: The engenhos of Pernambuco and Bahia made Brazil the world's sugar bowl — worked by enslaved labor.

title: The Iberian Union reaches Brazil
description: Portugal's crown passed to Philip II, exposing Brazil to Spain's enemies — above all the Dutch.

title: The Inquisition visits
description: A visitation of the Holy Office reached Bahia — colonial society examined and denounced neighbor by neighbor.

title: The bandeirantes push inland
description: São Paulo's expeditions probed the interior for captives and gold, dragging the map of Brazil westward.

title: France expelled from Maranhão
description: Portugal took São Luís and ended the second French Brazil.

title: The Dutch take Salvador
description: The West India Company seized the capital for a year — the opening blow of the Dutch war for Brazil.

title: Dutch Brazil
description: The Dutch took Pernambuco and held the sugar coast for a generation.

title: Maurits of Nassau governs Recife
description: The humanist governor brought painters and astronomers to Dutch Recife — the tropics' first scientific court.

title: Pernambuco rises
description: Planters rose against Dutch rule; ten years of guerrilla war followed.

title: The Dutch expelled
description: Recife capitulated and Dutch Brazil ended; the sugar war moved on to the Caribbean.

title: Peace with the Dutch
description: The Treaty of The Hague bought Dutch recognition of Portuguese Brazil for eight million florins.

title: Palmares defies destruction
description: The great quilombo of escaped slaves stood off repeated expeditions under its king Ganga Zumba.

title: The Beckman revolt
description: Maranhão planters overthrew the governor over the Jesuit-protected Indian labor laws.

title: Palmares falls
description: Bandeirante armies destroyed the quilombo; its last leader Zumbi fell a year later and became a legend.

title: Gold in Minas Gerais
description: Bandeirantes struck gold in the mountains; the greatest gold rush of the age emptied the coast and filled Lisbon.

title: Emperor without a coronation
description: Maximilian I took the title 'Elected Roman Emperor' without a papal crowning — Habsburg self-confidence codified.

title: Charles V's double inheritance
description: Spain, Burgundy, and the Austrian lands converged on one nineteen-year-old — the empire on which the sun never set.

title: Ferdinand takes the Austrian lands
description: Charles handed the hereditary lands to his brother — the founding split of the Austrian Habsburg line.

title: First siege of Vienna
description: Suleiman's army reached Vienna's walls and was turned back by autumn rains and stubborn defense.

title: Güns holds the road
description: A tiny fortress delayed the next Ottoman advance long enough for the campaign season to die.

title: Buda falls to the Ottomans
description: Hungary's capital became an Ottoman provincial seat; the Habsburg-Ottoman frontier froze across Hungary for 145 years.

title: The inheritance divided
description: Charles V abdicated, splitting Habsburg power: Spain to his son, the empire and Austria to his brother Ferdinand.

title: Maximilian II's quiet tolerance
description: The religiously ambiguous emperor granted his Protestant nobles freedom of worship on their estates.

title: Rudolf II moves the court to Prague
description: The melancholy emperor filled Prague castle with astronomers, alchemists, and art — Kepler would compute planetary orbits there.

title: The Long Turkish War
description: Thirteen years of border war with the Ottomans bled Hungary and ended in exhausted stalemate.

title: Peace of Zsitvatorok
description: The treaty ended the Long War — and for the first time addressed the Habsburg as an equal 'emperor', not a mere king.

title: The Defenestration of Prague
description: Bohemian Protestants threw two Habsburg governors from a castle window — the spark of the Thirty Years' War.

title: White Mountain
description: The Bohemian revolt was crushed in an hour outside Prague; the re-Catholicization of the Habsburg lands followed.

title: Wallenstein assassinated
description: The emperor's own generalissimo, grown too powerful and too independent, was murdered by his officers with imperial blessing.

title: Westphalia
description: The peace ended the Thirty Years' War, gutted imperial authority in Germany — and turned Habsburg ambition east and south.

title: Leopold I elected emperor
description: The musical, pious Leopold would reign nearly half a century and fight France and the Ottomans through most of it.

title: Victory at St. Gotthard
description: Montecuccoli's army stopped the Ottomans on the Raab — then Vienna signed a truce that gave back much of the gain.

title: The Vienna plague
description: Pestilence killed tens of thousands in Vienna; the survivors raised the Pestsäule that still stands on the Graben.

title: The second siege of Vienna
description: Kara Mustafa's great army broke against the city's walls and Sobieski's winged hussars — the Ottoman high-water mark.

title: Zenta
description: Prince Eugene destroyed the Ottoman army at the Tisza crossing; Hungary passed to the Habsburgs at Karlowitz two years later.

title: Leonardo begins the Mona Lisa
description: In Florence, Leonardo began the portrait he would carry with him for the rest of his life.

title: The Sistine ceiling unveiled
description: Michelangelo's four-year ordeal on the scaffold was revealed — the summit of Renaissance painting.

title: The Sack of Rome
description: Unpaid imperial troops sacked Rome for months; contemporaries read it as the end of the High Renaissance.

title: Florence's last republic falls
description: After a ten-month siege the republic surrendered and the Medici returned as hereditary dukes.

title: The Council of Trent opens
description: The council that answered the Reformation met in the Alpine border town of Trent, on and off for eighteen years.

title: Cateau-Cambrésis settles Italy
description: France renounced its Italian claims; Spain's dominance over the peninsula was sealed for a century and a half.

title: The Uffizi begun
description: Vasari began Cosimo's offices in Florence — the building that would become the world's first great art museum.

title: Lepanto
description: The Holy League's galleys — Venetian, papal, Spanish — broke Ottoman naval power in the last great oared battle.

title: The Gregorian calendar
description: Gregory XIII's reform dropped ten days from October — Rome resetting the clock of Christendom.

title: Caravaggio's Rome
description: The Contarelli Chapel canvases made Caravaggio famous overnight — light and violence remaking painting.

title: Giordano Bruno burned
description: The philosopher of infinite worlds was burned in the Campo de' Fiori, where his statue now faces the Vatican.

title: Galileo's telescope
description: The Starry Messenger announced Jupiter's moons and mountains on our own — the sky would never be the same.

title: St. Peter's consecrated
description: After 120 years of building under twenty popes, the new basilica was consecrated.

title: Galileo's trial
description: The Inquisition forced the old astronomer to abjure Copernicanism — 'and yet it moves', the legend answers.

title: Masaniello's revolt
description: A Naples fishmonger led a tax revolt that ruled the city for days — and was murdered within the week.

title: Plague devastates Naples
description: The epidemic killed perhaps half of Europe's largest city in a single summer.

title: Venice loses Crete
description: Candia fell after a twenty-one-year siege — the longest in history — ending Venice's greatest colony.

title: Messina revolts against Spain
description: The Sicilian city called in French help against its Spanish rulers; France abandoned it four years later.

title: A Venetian shell hits the Parthenon
description: Morosini's mortars ignited the Ottoman powder store inside the temple — antiquity's most famous ruin made in one blast.

title: Sicily's great earthquake
description: The quake leveled Catania and its neighbors; the rebuilding created the late-baroque towns of the Val di Noto.

title: The Austrian lands divided
description: Ferdinand I's death split the hereditary lands among three sons — Vienna, Graz, and Innsbruck each with its own Habsburg court.
